Takeaway: When you spot a pawn defended by a piece tied to another job (like the knight guarding e6 *and* h3), look for ways to exploit that overload—it’s often the key to turning a positional edge into something concrete.

I'd play Qxa8. You spotted the hanging rook on a8—great instinct—but Ng4 was a detour. By taking the rook immediately you open the back rank for your queen, win material cleanly, and sidestep Black’s g-file counterplay. The upside is far greater than the downside: Black’s checks are harmless once you’ve scooped the rook, and the bishop on c8 is trapped after Qxa7.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended major piece on the opponent’s back rank, ask yourself if any of your threats (checks, discovered attacks, or mate ideas) are truly dangerous—if not, grab it at once.

I'd play Re8+.

You had a great instinct to target the loose d4-pawn, but capturing it right away lets White’s queen step in and defend everything. Check first: Re8+ forces the king to move (only Kf1 is safe), and now the bishop on c8 suddenly joins the attack. The instant White runs the king, you win the d4-pawn anyway, but with an extra tempo—so your rook stays active and the opponent doesn’t get time to untangle.

Takeaway: When you see a loose pawn, ask “Can I add one more threat before I grab it?”—often a simple check or pin pays for itself.

I'd play f5. Takeaway: When an enemy piece stretches toward your camp, look first to push or kick it before you recapture.

I'd play f5. You saw the undefended pawn on a2 and went for the grab, but look at the big picture: White’s rook on e4 is just sitting there, eyeing your kingside. Pushing f5 chases that rook away and lets you follow up by swinging your queen to f6, where she both defends your kingside and eyes the knight on f3. I'd play Re8+.

Your move Bxd4 grabs a pawn, but it lets White’s queen escape to e1 and keeps the position messy. The concrete plan is simple: after the king moves, you can snatch the d4 pawn with your bishop while White is still scrambling. This also keeps your pieces active and ties White’s queen to defensive duties, preventing them from launching their own counterplay.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s king is exposed, look for checks that force weaknesses before grabbing material—activity often beats small gains.

I'd play Bb4+.

You saw the knight coming to d7, but missed the power of starting with check. Bb4+ chases the king away from the center, which often wins time to bring your other pieces into the game—here it threatens to discovered-attack the queen with your rook on f8, and the extra move can let you grab the pawn on g2 next turn if White isn’t careful. The student’s move was solid development, but a quick check would have put you a step ahead.

I'd play Re8+.

It also brings your rook into the game with a clear job—pressuring the e-file where White’s king just fled. You essentially get the same pawn back, plus the king is now out in the open, which can lead to bigger chances later.

I'd play Bb4+.

Once White scrambles to deal with the check, your knight can safely jump to d7 next move while their pieces are still in disarray.

I'd play **Bb4+**.

You picked Nbd7, which is solid, but it lets White's queen keep eyeing f7 and c7 without any real pressure. With Bb4+ you do three things at once: force the White king into the pawn cover on f1 (no easy castling), threaten to chop the queen on e5 if the king runs to d1, and keep your own queen active. It’s the kind of move that transforms a slightly awkward position into a coordinated initiative, and at this level you’re absolutely ready to spot these checks that tie the opponent’s king down.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s king is still in the center, a well-timed check often reveals squares you can exploit before they coordinate their pieces.

I'd play Qh2. Instead of tucking the king away, trading queens solves two problems at once: it removes Black's dangerous queen that's eyeing your kingside, and it activates your own queen without risk. Your move fixes the awkward placement of your pieces—right now, your queen is just sitting there while Black’s knight on e3 and queen on h3 make threats. After Qh2 Qxh2+ Kxh2, the board feels calmer, and you can focus on improving your other pieces.

Takeaway: When you see a safe queen trade that removes your opponent's active pieces, go for it—it often simplifies the game to your advantage.

I'd play f5.

You’re absolutely right to pounce on loose stuff—grabbing that pawn on a2 feels natural. The problem is it lets White rally: his rook drops to a1, your queen gets chased, and suddenly your bishop on a6 is hanging and your coordination is shot. Instead, kick the rook right now with f5. That rook is overextended and has nowhere good to go; after it steps back you can calmly bring the queen to f6, trading off White’s best minor piece (the knight on f3) and shielding your pawn weaknesses. You keep all your pieces working together and turn his rook into a liability instead of a hero.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s piece is out on a limb, look first for ways to attack it before cashing in on side pawns.

I'd play Qh2. Your king move was safe, but it misses a chance to trade queens on Black's terms—here, Black’s queen is both pressing on h3 and close to your king, so swapping it off eases the pressure right away. After Qh2 Qxh2+ you’ve traded the most active enemy piece, your king is no longer in the crosshairs, and you can calmly roll your central pawns forward. It’s a simple, proactive step that keeps the initiative without any risk.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s queen is both aggressive and undefended, look for a queen trade that improves your position for free.

I'd play Qh2. You noticed the king was a little exposed and rightly wanted to tuck it behind the rook, but here the real issue is Black’s queen hovering on h3. Swapping queens while you still have pawns around to shield your king kills the only real threat Black had, and the resulting endgame is dead even. You’ll trade actively, keep your pieces happy, and never worry about a cheap shot on g2 again.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s queen is the only attacker, look first for a safe queen trade.

I'd play c4. You grabbed the c6 pawn right away, but Black can just take back and activate their rooks. Instead, push that pawn to c4—it directly fights for the d5 pawn and keeps your rook on its strong outpost while inviting Black’s pieces to awkward squares. This move sets up a clear plan: trade or chase the d5 pawn to open more lines for your rooks, which are already well-placed.

Takeaway: When you have a strong rook on an open file, use pawn breaks to open more lines instead of grabbing material that lets the opponent activate their pieces.

I'd play c4. You saw the pressure on the d5 pawn, but Rxc6 just trades off your active rook for a pawn—Black’s doubled c-pawns aren’t weak yet, and your rook on b6 is doing great work. Instead, c4 keeps all your pieces in the game and forces Black to react: if they push d5-d4, you’ll have a beautiful outpost on d4 for your knight; if they trade on c4, you’ll recapture with the pawn and open lines for both rooks.

Takeaway: When you spot a pawn weakness, look first for pawn breaks that keep your pieces active rather than trading them away.

I'd play Bd1. You saw a threat to your bishop and rushed to defend it with the king, but that actually walks into a worse pin on the h1-rook. Instead, swing the bishop back to d1 so it defends both the f3-pawn and keeps the back rank safe—now your king can breathe and you’re ready to kick that annoying knight away with f4 later.

I'd play c4.

You took the pawn on c6 with Rxc6, which looks flashy but actually gives Black a little breathing room. By playing c4 instead, you put immediate pressure on their d5 pawn—their whole queenside is weak and this forces them to react. If they push d4, you can play c5 and start rolling your pawns, or if they retreat, your rook on b6 keeps eyeing the weak pawns on a6 and c6. The idea is simple: instead of grabbing material right away, create tension and make Black defend passively.

Takeaway: When you have rooks on open or half-open files, look for pawn breaks that open target squares before grabbing material.

I'd play Bd1. You noticed the loose pawn on f3 and wanted to protect it with Kf2—that’s careful thinking—but after Black takes on h1 the queen will escape and you’re just down a rook. Instead, bring the bishop back to d1 where it stays safe, guards f3, and nudges Black’s queen to move again. I'd play Bd1. You spotted that your bishop is loose and the f3-pawn is hanging, but Kf2 only addresses half the problem—it leaves the queen still staring down the h-file. By moving the bishop to d1, you defend f3 *and* connect the rooks, which lets you safely tuck the king to f2 next move. That tiny shuffle fixes both loose points at once and keeps the queen safe.

Takeaway: When two of your pieces are loose, look for a single move that defends both before you move the king.

I'd play Re8.

You grabbed the c3 pawn with your queen, which isn’t bad—it’s safe and keeps material even—but Re8 is stronger because it sets up a clear, active plan. By swinging the rook to e8, you immediately threaten the queen on e4, forcing your opponent to react. Instead of just defending your loose knight or pawn, you start creating counterplay by putting pressure on their queen, which ties them down to saving it. At your level, it’s more important to build active threats than to scoop up small pawns.

Takeaway: When your opponent has an undefended piece, look for direct threats first—don’t just take pawns unless they solve a bigger problem.

I'd play Bf3. You spotted the loose pawn on b7, and that’s exactly the kind of small target a beginner should train their eyes on—it’s attacked only by your queen right now, and adding another attacker with Bf3 wins it cleanly on the next move. The whole idea is simple: pile on anything Black can’t defend quickly, then take the free material.

Takeaway: When you see an opponent’s piece or pawn left unprotected, look for the easiest way to attack it again.

I'd play Re8. You spotted the hanging queen, but capturing the pawn on c3 lets White trade queens next move and keep the firepower. Instead, simply move the rook to e8 to attack the queen directly—if she runs, you win material cleanly, and if she trades, you recapture with the rook and keep your queen for the later fight. This punishes White’s loose pieces with a simple, forcing idea you’ll see again and again.

Takeaway: When the enemy queen is undefended on an open file, lining up a rook behind yours is often the cleanest way to win it.

I'd play **g4**.

You saw a nice rook lift with Rd1, but the position is too sharp for slow moves—Black looks ready to break with …c5 or …fxe5 any second. By playing g4 right now, you kick the bishop away and open the g-file for your rook before Black can grab the knight on e5. Takeaway: When you spot an enemy piece eyeing a key square, ask if you can chase it away *before* they can use it as a springboard.

I'd play Bf3. Your move Rd1 is fine, but it just nudges the queen without improving White’s pieces—Bf3 instead **ramps up the pressure** on b7 while keeping a rock-solid kingside. The bishop eyes that loose pawn, the g2 pawn stays defended, and you’re ready to swing a rook to d1 or d3 next turn if Black tries to wiggle out. I'd play Re8. You already spotted the hanging pawn on c3, but trading queens here lets White activate their rook on a1 and leave you with a passive rook and no real counterplay. Re8 instead challenges White’s queen right away—they’ll have to move it, and you can then follow up with ideas like Qh4 or c5 to create threats while keeping your knight alive. Takeaway: When you have a cramped position, look first for ways to activate your pieces rather than trading—especially when the trade eases your opponent’s coordination.

I'd play Qxd5.

The queen can take it instead, and suddenly you’re attacking the rook on a8 *and* keeping an eye on Black’s other pieces. From d5 your queen also helps protect your own pawn on e4, so you don’t have to worry about it dropping off next move.

I'd play Qxd5. You saw each side only has one loose piece: your pawn on e4 and Black’s bishop on d5. Instead of trading pawn for bishop (which leaves the whole d-file open for Black’s rooks), take with the queen and instantly threaten both the undefended rook on a8 and the b5 pawn. Black has to scramble, and you can calmly retreat to d2 next move, connecting the rooks and keeping an extra pawn with no risk.

Takeaway: When both sides have only one hanging piece, recapture with the stronger piece to pile on new threats instead of just restoring material.

I'd play Be6. You spotted the knight and pawn on f7 need cover, but the real target here is that undefended a2 pawn—it’s screaming to be taken. Moving the bishop to e6 hits it immediately while also reinforcing f7, and if White chases the bishop with g4 you can calmly pick up the pawn later after b6. I'd play Qa6. Now you keep all your pieces safe and threaten White’s b-pawn, showing how sideways queen moves can solve problems without giving up activity.

I'd play Be6. You noticed the knight on e8 needs defending, but right now the queen on e5 is also eying the bishop on c4 and your pawn on f7—both are loose. By swinging the bishop to e6, you cover f7 again *and* hit the weak pawn on a2 at the same time, giving your opponent two things to worry about instead of just reacting to your king move. Takeaway: When the opponent is eyeing your weak spots, look for a single move that defends what you care about *and* attacks something of theirs.

I'd play Qxd5. You missed a great chance to trade your queen for Black’s bishop and rook—a classic “minor plus rook” upgrade that nets you an extra pawn and keeps all your pieces alive. This one-two punch is a classic pattern: trade a bishop for two pawns and open the f-file, then swing the queen to h5 to threaten mate on f7.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s king is still in the center, look for forcing checks to open lines before worrying about side pawns.

I'd play Nxg5.

You saw the queen on d5 and wanted to grab something, but snatching the pawn on c7 let Black trade queens and develop easily. Instead, Qa4+ forces Black’s king to move or blocks it with a piece, and the check also threatens to win the pawn on a7 afterward. It’s a simple way to keep pressure while giving yourself options—like bringing the rook to d1 next move to pile on.

Takeaway: When you see a queen staring at the enemy king, look for a safe check before grabbing material.

I'd play Rxb6.

You saw the rook on f2 was hanging and grabbed it with your queen, but Black’s queen can recapture and suddenly White’s rook on b4 is loose—your big targets just disappeared. Instead, use the geometry of the board: your rook on b4 already attacks b6, and the pawn there shields e6. Take it, and Black’s pawns start cracking; your rook on a1 can swing to the open file next move for a clear plan.

Takeaway: When you spot one loose enemy pawn, look for a second target behind it before you grab the first.

I'd play Qa4+.

You wanted to win a pawn, but grabbing the c7 pawn let Black’s queen slip away and gave their king a free tempo. With Qa4+ you actually *force* their king to stay in the center, where it’s awkward and blocks their rook from connecting, while also threatening the a7 pawn—a much safer way to keep winning chances. After the check, every square the king goes to leaves a new weakness: Ke7 blocks the bishop on f8, Kd7 lets you swing a rook to d1 with real pressure.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s king is still in the center, a check often does more good than a quick pawn grab.

I'd play Qa4+.

You saw the opportunity to grab the pawn on c7, but after Black trades queens and plays Bd7, the threat of Rb8 followed by pressure on the b-file becomes awkward to handle—your queen gets tangled up defending too many things. Qa4+ is a clean forcing move: it chases the king, which can’t castle safely anymore, and sets up a discovered attack on the queen after Bd7. The check also keeps your queen active while avoiding trades, so you maintain coordination and can follow up with Rd1 to pile on the d-file.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s queen is centralized, checks that force the king to move often reveal hidden pressure on the queen herself.

I'd play **g4**.

You played a check that feels active, but it just lets Black’s queen settle on a strong central square (d4) without any real pressure. Instead, g4 cuts right to the heart of the position: Black’s pawn on f4 is the only thing holding the queenside together, and pushing g4 undermines it immediately. If Black takes, you recapture with the h-pawn, fixing your kingside pawns and opening the g-file for your rook, while if they retreat, the f4 pawn becomes a long-term weakness you can attack later.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a single advanced pawn propping up their position, look for the pawn break that directly challenges it.

I'd play Rfe8. Your idea with g6 was to kick the pawn, but White can just push it farther with f6, and suddenly our kingside is full of holes. By moving the rook to e8 instead, you keep everything defended—your bishop on e7, your other rook—and you stay flexible to react to whatever White does next. It’s a quiet move that keeps you safe and ready.

Takeaway: When you’re not in trouble, reinforce your position before making aggressive moves.

I'd play g3.

You spotted the check, which is great—keeping your queen active is always a good thought. But here, Black’s pawn on f4 is just hanging, and g3 cuts off its support while also kicking it backward. The idea is simple: trade that pawn for one of yours, open the g-file for your rook, and keep pressing without letting Black’s queen get too comfortable. You missed that White can win a pawn right away, and g3 does exactly that in a calm, safe way.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended enemy pawn, first ask which of your pawns can attack it safely.

I'd play **e5**.

You missed a great chance to strike back with energy. The pawn on d4 is only defended once, so pushing e5 attacks it and also shields your pawn on f4—two threats solved at once. After the pawn moves, your pieces suddenly breathe: the knight eyes better squares, and your queen can swing toward the kingside to challenge White’s queen. You played fxe3 too early; it gave up a strong pawn wedge and let White keep the initiative without a fight.

Takeaway: When you’re under pressure, look for central pawn breaks that solve multiple problems at once.

I'd play Bd8. The pawn on g5 is a target, and moving your bishop to d8 attacks it while keeping your position solid. This also opens the door for a pawn push to f6, kicking that knight away and giving your pieces more space to breathe—something you missed by playing g6, which doesn’t challenge White’s pieces directly. The bishop move is quiet but sets up a clear plan: improve your outposts before worrying about pawn breaks.

Takeaway: When ahead, look first to challenge your opponent’s active pieces before making pawn moves.

I'd play **e5**.

You played fxe3, which grabs a pawn right away, but White’s queen is lurking on h8 and can swoop down to h7, attacking your king. Pushing e5 instead does two things at once: it plants a pawn firmly in the center and blocks the queen from reaching h7. That gives you time to calmly tuck your own queen to e8 next, covering the seventh rank and shielding the kingside.

Takeaway: When facing a queen on the edge of the board, first block its entry route before snatching side pawns.

I'd play g4. You rightly wanted to put pressure on Black’s king, but checks like Qb2+ just let the queen trade herself off—the very piece that should be hunting. g4 immediately stamps out any counterplay on the f-file and shuts Black’s rooks out of e5 or g5; after they take on g3 you recapture with the h-pawn, leaving them with ugly pawn islands while you keep a firm grip on the dark squares. It’s the kind of sharp pawn stab that wins games at your level once you trust it.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s king is loose, push pawns to restrict their pieces instead of checking away your biggest attacker.

I'd play Bh6. You grabbed the e3 pawn to “win material,” but White’s queen is aiming right at your king while your own pieces are still scattered—cutting that queen off before she lands on h7 is the real punch. Bh6 plants a piece where it blocks the check, defends your loose f4 pawn, and threatens to sneak the queen over to g8 next move, so White has to waste time saving the queen instead of hunting your king. You keep the pawn, keep the initiative, and turn the tables on the pin.

Takeaway: Before snapping up a pawn, ask if your king is safe and your pieces are working together—if not, develop first.

I'd play Qa2. You played g6, which does kick the pawn, but it ignores a bigger idea: pressuring White’s weak a3 pawn while keeping your queen active. Qa2 attacks a3 right away, and if White isn’t careful, you can swing the queen to f7 or even take on a3 later—all while still eyeing the knight on e4. It’s a simple way to mix threats and keep White guessing.

Takeaway: At this level, hunt weak pawns first—especially edge pawns—before making pawn moves that don’t directly challenge your opponent.

I'd play c4.

You spotted that Black’s queen is loose on b5—that’s great tactical awareness! Instead of jumping straight to Qxf6 (which just trades queens and lets Black’s rooks wake up), c4 chases the queen away while keeping your own queen active. It also prepares to follow up with moves like Rbe1, keeping pressure on the whole board. This teaches you to ask: “Before I trade, can I make my opponent move first and gain space?”

Takeaway: Look for ways to push a piece away before capturing—you’ll often get a better deal.

I'd play Rxe5.

Great job spotting that the bishop on e5 is unprotected—you’re already thinking about captures! However, swapping your queen for their bishop with Qxe5 or sending out your knight for a quick snack with Nxe5 gives Black easy play. Instead, use your rook: it’s safer, keeps your queen free to pressure f7, and sticks to a simple plan—trade a rook for their good bishop and then put more pieces on the open e-file.

Takeaway: When you spot an unprotected piece, first look for your least valuable attacker to grab it.

I'd play c4. The queen on b5 is awkwardly placed defending Black’s rook, so kicking her with c4 forces her to retreat. That clears e2 for your rook to swing over next move, while also making sure you keep pressure on the queenside—exactly the kind of small positional nudge that builds into a lasting edge. You took f6, which wins a pawn, but here you can gain space and coordination without rushing.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s queen is on a square that defends too many things, look for pawn breaks that attack it and open lines for your own pieces.

I'd play Qxe5. That bold queen sally grabs the bishop, and—just as important—creates a clear target: Black’s stranded rook on h8. Your knight on e5 would have won the bishop too, but after …Qf5 the queen trade leaves Black’s rook still looking silly on h8 with no good way back; keeping the queen on the board forces Black to waste time untangling.

Takeaway: When you're up material, look for moves that give the opponent awkward pieces to juggle—often the queen can both win and pin.

I'd play Qd7. You found Rc8, which looks active, but it actually just loses your rook after White takes your queen and then your rook. Qd7 is much safer—it keeps your queen defended and chases the knight away, which also frees your bishop on g7 to breathe. If White’s knight moves, you can calmly bring your other rook to c8 next move with no risk.

Takeaway: Always ask, “What’s endangered?” before moving—your queen and rook were both loose, and Qd7 fixes both.

I'd play c4.

You took on f6 because it looked safe, but the queen trade freed Black’s rook and gave her active play without much to show for it. Instead, c4 kicks the queen off her prime diagonal and sets the simple plan “push the pawn, then swing the rooks.” The move also keeps your own queen connected to d4 so Black can’t bite with …Qxb3. At your level, spotting a single pawn push that does two useful things at once is the kind of “power move” you’ll want to stock in your toolbox.

Takeaway: When you can attack an enemy piece while improving your own pawn structure, do it—one move, two jobs.

I'd play Rxe5. The knight on c4 is already doing a great job — it hits d6, eyes e5, and keeps Black’s pieces tied down. By bringing the rook to e5 instead, you double the pressure on that square, threaten to roll the rook immediately to e1 with tempo against the knight on e7, and keep your queen and bishop flexible for follow-up checks or pins. The student’s Nxe5 is fine, but it trades off the most active minor piece for a temporary pin that Black can wiggle out of; Rxe5 keeps more pieces on the board and turns the e-file into a lasting highway for your rooks.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s bishop is the “glue” that holds their position together, try trading with a rook first — it often sets up a stronger follow-up than a knight capture.

I'd play Qd7. You rushed with Rc8, which swaps rook for queen—but that leaves White’s powerful knight on e6 unchallenged. Qd7 pins the knight to its king, threatens f5-f4 undermining the bishop on g5, and keeps your heavy pieces connected along the back rank. If White trades queens, recapture with the rook, and that same knight will drop next.

Takeaway: At this level, focus first on threats that force concessions, not just trades.

I'd play Qd7. The key here is to target the knight on e6, which is doing a lot for White—attacking your f8-rook and eyeing your king—but isn’t actually defended. Rc8 just let White swap queens on c8 and take your rook next move, trading too much for too little. Qd7 keeps pressure on that knight while also covering your rook on f8, so if White tries Nxf8, you recapture with your bishop and hold the pawn on d6. It’s a calm way to keep the game in your hands without giving anything away.

Takeaway: When a piece is both active and undefended, look first for quiet queen moves that attack it while covering your own loose pieces.

I'd play Bd6. You spotted that the White rook on e5 is free, but grabbing the f2 pawn is too hasty—it lets White push the passed d-pawn toward promotion. Instead, by moving the bishop to d6 you chase the rook away and keep everything protected: your pawn on c7, your other rook, and your king. After the rook moves, you can calmly complete your development and turn your extra central pawn into a real advantage.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended piece, ask yourself “who defends what,” not just “can I take it?”—often the bigger chance is to attack and gain influence.

I'd play Rxa2.

Your move, Rd8, was a reasonable try to activate your rook, but it missed a big opportunity to grab material right away. White’s a-pawn is undefended, and your rook can take it safely—after Rxa2, your rook still guards your own a-pawn, so you’re just up a pawn for free. This also keeps pressure on White’s weak g-pawn, which you can target next with your other rook or king.

Takeaway: Always check for undefended pawns—if your piece can take one and keep defending your own, it’s usually worth doing.

I'd play Bd6.

You grabbed the pawn on f2, but that move actually lets White push c4 next, gaining space and breathing room. Instead, Bd6 kicks the rook away and keeps your pieces active—your bishop stays defended, your rook on f8 gets more freedom, and you start building pressure down the e-file. It’s a simple, strong switch: trade a quick pawn for a clear plan.

Takeaway: Always ask “what’s my opponent’s best reply?” before snatching an undefended pawn.

I'd play Rxa2.

You saw the hanging pawn on a2, but missed that after grabbing it your rook instantly attacks another one on g2 while keeping your own a7 pawn safe. The fork on the pawns gives you time to activate your other rook next move and start pressing down the board—no need to retreat the way Rd8 did. Taking with the rook also keeps your pawn structure flexible so you can push either b7-b5 or a7-a5 later.

Takeaway: When you spot an undefended piece, ask what else your capturing piece will attack as soon as it lands.

I'd play Bd6. The rook on e5 looks mighty, but it's actually somewhat loose—your bishop can chase it away, and in the process you activate your own pieces. By playing Bd6, you not only attack the rook, but you also prepare to develop your light-squared bishop to f5 or g4, putting more pressure on White's position. This move fixes your student move by avoiding a superficial capture and instead focusing on a clear, strong plan that improves your position step by step.

Takeaway: When you see a strong-looking enemy piece, ask yourself if it’s truly defended—often, a simple attack can disrupt their plans and give you the initiative.

I'd play Qd7.

Your move Qxe7 was fine, but it trades your strong queen for a bishop that was already trapped. Instead, Qd7 saves your queen *and* attacks White’s queen at the same time—simple double duty! You keep your pieces safe while putting pressure on White to defend, which is exactly what beginners should practice looking for.

Takeaway: When your queen is attacked, look for a move that protects her *and* eyes another enemy piece.

I'd play Rxa2. You saw the hanging pawn on a2 but backed away; grabbing it actually kicks off a simple but powerful plan: trade the rook on f1 to relieve pressure, then bring the other rook to the open e-file and hunt down the stray knight that’s trapped behind enemy lines. The trick is to stay active—you’re a pawn up and can afford the tempo if you keep coordinating your rooks.

Takeaway: When you’re up material, trade down aggressively to convert rather than letting your opponent rebuild their play.

I'd play Qd7. You spotted the loose bishop on e7, which is great—that’s exactly the kind of opportunity intermediate players should pounce on. But taking it with the queen lets White’s knight hop into d2, eyeing your queen and queenside, which can quickly get uncomfortable. Qd7 instead chases the bishop *and* eyes White’s queen, forcing her to retreat or trade, while your own queen stays safe on d7. It’s a neat way to keep the pressure without walking into a fork.

Takeaway: When your queen and another piece both attack the same loose enemy piece, try moving the queen to add one more threat—often you’ll win material for free.

I'd play Qxf3. Instead of wandering your knight to the rim where it blocks your own pawns, use the chance to win a pawn right away—your queen eyes f3, and White’s pawn is stuck there with no defenders. Capturing on f3 keeps the pressure on; you’ll still attack the pawns on d3 and e4, and White can’t easily kick your queen away without giving you more.

Takeaway: At this level, count defenders first—if a pawn or piece has none, look for the simplest way to grab it without hanging your own stuff.

I'd play Qxf3. You spotted the loose pawn on f3—but instead of sending your knight on a slow trip, use the queen's power right away: take the pawn, threaten the pawn on d3, and keep pressure on the center. Your knight on c6 is actually doing a great job defending your own pawn on b7, so moving it away just gave White a free tempo.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended enemy pawn, check first if your biggest pieces can grab it before sending a minor piece on a detour.

I'd play Qxd4.

You grabbed the bishop with your pawn, which looks safe—but actually, your queen is now doing too much work alone. By trading queens right away, you keep control of the center and remove Black’s active pieces. The opponent’s rook is eyeing your h-pawn through the half-open h-file, but after the queen trade, your pawn on e3 safely recaptures, and you can calmly push c5 next to activate your bishop.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a loose piece and active pieces, prioritize trading your own less-flexible pieces to reduce their firepower.

I'd play Qxd4.

You spotted the loose bishop on d4, which is great—pointing out hanging pieces is exactly what you should be doing at every move. But instead of recapturing with the pawn, think about bringing your strongest piece into the action: the queen. Qxd4 not only grabs that bishop but also eyes the weak pawn on d5 and keeps an eye on Black’s queen on b6, which ties Black’s pieces down. The pawn recapture you chose, exd4, lets Black’s rook slide to h4 and start chipping away at your pawn shield, a plan that’s easy for them to find and annoying to defend against.

Takeaway: Always ask, “Can my strongest piece join the fight here?” before trading minor pieces.

I'd play Qxf3. You missed a chance to punish White’s overextended pawn shield: the f3 pawn is undefended and blocks the queen’s guard of d3. Capturing it opens two lines at once—your queen eyes d3 and e4, while your rooks can swing to e-file next move. The plan is simple: grab the pawn, then double on the e-file to target e4.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s pawn chain points toward your king but lacks defenders, look for the base pawn you can snatch to undermine the whole structure.

I'd play Qxd4.

You went for the pawn recapture exd4, which is natural, but it lets Black grab the h4-pawn next move, giving away a healthy kingside pawn with little compensation. The real target here is Black’s bishop on d4—it’s undefended and sitting on a diagonal that also eyes your queenside. Trading queens right away keeps the pressure on, keeps your structure solid, and gives you a slight but clear edge without any risk. It’s a reminder that when your opponent’s piece is hanging, the queen isn’t just the strongest piece—she’s often the most efficient recapturer.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s piece is undefended, look first at how the queen can profit from the trade—she’s often worth more than the material alone.

I'd play Rg3.

You spotted the loose pawn on h3 and wanted to tuck the king away, but moving the rook instead solves two problems at once. Rg3 swings the heavy piece toward Black’s queen, threatening to embarrass her next move—if the queen flees to h5, your queen can simply slide to e4 and cover everything. At the same time, the rook defends h3 without needing the king to run, so you keep your extra pawn and stay on the attack. You already noticed the h3 weakness; now just use the rook that’s begging to join the party.

Takeaway: When an undefended piece is hanging, look first for a single move that both attacks an enemy piece and defends your own.

I'd play Qd7.

You took the bishop right away, but the real target was the queen on f5—she’s the one causing all the trouble by eyeing both your king and pawn on d5. Moving your queen to d7 does three good things at once: it kicks White’s queen, protects your own bishop on f7 (so the bishop doesn’t hang the next move), and keeps an eye on your d5 pawn so it doesn’t fall after Qxf5. This move keeps the tension and gives White something uncomfortable to solve instead of trading down for free.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s queen is eyeing your king, look for ways to kick her without trading—often you’ll find a quiet move that keeps more pieces on the board and preserves real pressure.

I'd play Rg3.

You noticed the loose pawn on h3 and played Kh1 to guard it, which is careful — that’s good! But look at the whole board: the real problem is Black’s queen and knight teaming up on h3 and g2, and the queen even threatens to swing over to c2. Rg3 cuts off the queen’s escape route and lets your queen join the defence without hiding your king. It’s a simple idea: **use your rook to chase the enemy queen away** while keeping everything safe.

Takeaway: When an enemy piece is hitting two of your things, look for a single move that defends both at once.

I'd play Kxf6.

The beginner rush to “pawn takes piece” is natural, but in this position your king can do three jobs at once: remove the bishop that’s eyeing your rook on h8, defend your e5 pawn so it doesn’t hang, and also keep control of e6 so your own bishop stays safe. Stepping toward the center with Kxf6 is safe, simple, and leaves White’s queen and rooks looking awkward.

Takeaway: When an enemy piece is both attacking you and undefended, ask “Can my king clean it up while helping my position?”

I'd play Kxf6.

You picked gxf6, which is okay, but it lets White’s queen swing to b7 with check and keeps your own king stuck in the center. Kxf6 instead steals the bishop, keeps your pawn on g7 defended, and lets your king step quietly toward f7—right behind the safety of your pawns. It also sets up a simple plan: if White chases with the queen, you can calmly block with Bc7 and keep improving your position.

Takeaway: When a piece both attacks and defends at once, ask if the king can do the job more actively than a pawn.

I'd play Rg3. You missed a chance to create real pressure by driving Black’s queen away from the defense of the kingside light squares—right now she’s staring at your own queen on c6, so a rook lift to g3 forces her to step back and weakens the dark squares around Black’s king. After she moves, your queen can swing over to e4 or h3, threatening mate or picking off the loose pawn on h6. Instead of tucking the king, this keeps the initiative and turns Black’s aggressive knight on h4 into a bystander.

Takeaway: When your opponent lands a piece aggressively but has no follow-up, look for a zwischenzug that forces their queen to move and exposes their king.

I'd play Kxf6. You saw the hanging bishop and grabbed it with the pawn, but the real point is to use your king actively—Black’s pawn shield is already weakened, so tucking the king to f6 (then f7) keeps it safe while trading off White’s powerful bishop and steadily improving your position. The pawn on f6 can wait; a king that helps defend key squares like e6 and g7 is far more valuable at your level.

Takeaway: When the opponent leaves a piece unprotected, first check whether trading it with your king improves your safety before recapturing with a pawn.

I'd play Qxd2+.

Your move, Bxe6, is perfectly fine, but it misses a golden opportunity to kickstart your development with a concrete gain. The white queen on d2 is *hanging*—no pieces are guarding her—and by taking her with your queen, you not only win material but also force white to recapture with the king, which keeps their rook on h1 out of the game for a while. After swapping queens, your bishop is still eyeing the pawn on f7, so you can safely develop it next move without worrying about tactics. This sequence gives you a head start in piece activity while keeping things simple and forcing.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s piece is undefended and you can take it safely, look for a forcing sequence like a check to gain time.

I'd play h4. You played h3, which just waits — but right now Black’s pawn on g5 is hanging, and after h4 it has to move or be taken. This keeps the pressure on and gives our rooks more open files to join the fight. It’s a simple, active pawn push that actually creates a threat instead of just marking time.

Takeaway: Always ask, “What does my move actually do?” — if the answer is “nothing,” look for a pawn or piece that can poke or prod.

I'd play Qxd2+. The student’s move, Bxe6, is fine, but trading queens right now is far more instructive for an intermediate player. After Qxd2+ the position suddenly becomes much simpler: White’s active pieces disappear, Black’s king is safer, and the weak pawn on f7 is now defended by the rook. If White tries to recapture with the king, the bishop on e6 hangs and we calmly take it next move, keeping all the small advantages we’ve collected.

Takeaway: When you can trade queens in an open position and your opponent’s king looks exposed, the exchange often clears the board and makes your job easier.

I'd play Qxd2+. You already spotted that the bishop on e6 is hanging, but trading queens first is stronger because it keeps the game simple and avoids giving White any counterplay. After Qxd2+, White has to recapture, and then Bxe6 leaves you a clean pawn up with a safe king and active pieces—exactly the kind of position an advanced player should convert without drama. The extra tempo you gain by checking before capturing also helps you consolidate quickly, making it harder for White to create threats.

Takeaway: When you see a hanging piece, ask if trading down first can make your advantage even clearer.

I'd play Rd7. You spotted that Black’s pawn on b6 is loose, but grabbing it with Rxb6 runs into Rc1+ followed by Rxf2, giving Black too much activity. Instead, swing the rook to d7, hitting the g7 pawn while keeping an eye on b6—Black can’t defend both. This move also nudges their rook off the open file, leaving you in charge of the seventh rank.

Takeaway: When an undefended pawn tempts you, first check if attacking a *second* weakness forces Black to choose which one to save.

I'd play **Kf7**.

Instead of tucking the king away where it doesn’t help, Kf7 keeps it active while defending your bishop on f6. From f7, the king supports a simple pawn break: later you can push your b-pawn to b4 or even trade on c4, opening lines to attack White’s weak pawns. It’s a calm, flexible move that lets you stay in control.

Takeaway: Always ask, “What does this move help me do next?”—the best move often does more than just defend.

I'd play Rd7. You spotted the loose pawn on b6, but Black's rooks are actually almost connected—so grabbing it immediately lets the rooks double and swarm your back rank. Instead, Rd7 attacks the g7 pawn, which is still defended only by the king; if Black trades rooks on d7 you recapture with your king and then calmly take b6, while if he moves the rook on f8 you can slide to d4 or e7 with extra threats. It turns a one-shot tactic into a little forcing sequence that keeps Black on the defensive.

Takeaway: Against loose pawns, first improve the worst-placed piece—often that’s enough to win the pawn under better conditions.

I'd play **Kf7**. The rook on f2 is under attack, and moving your king to f7 not only saves it but also keeps your bishop on f6 defended—it’s a natural, active square for the king. This simple move also prepares to push your central pawns (like ...e4) later, giving you more space and activity. You want your king to be part of the solution, not just hiding.

Takeaway: When a piece is threatened, ask: “Can my king step in to help, AND set up something active next?”

I'd play Kf7. Your Kh8 is safe, but it lets White swap rooks and pull the game toward a quiet, even position—exactly what they want after grabbing that extra pawn. Kf7 keeps tension on the board and sets up a clear next step: advance the e-pawn to challenge White’s center, while your king shields the bishop on f6 and eyes the weak d5 pawn. That little king step also keeps both rooks connected so you can bring the rook on a8 into the fight later.

Takeaway: When ahead, activate your pieces instead of passively defending—your king can be a useful helper.

I'd play Ne7. Great job spotting the undefended bishop on h8, but grabbing it lets White's queen jump to g5, which suddenly eyes your king and threatens checkmate—definitely not what we want! Instead, Ne7 chases that same bishop away *and* bolsters your d5 pawn, keeping your position safe and solid while improving your pieces. It’s a simple, safe way to get organized while keeping White on the back foot.

Takeaway: Always ask, “What’s the simplest way to defend my weak spots and upgrade my worst-placed piece?”—it often beats grabbing material.

I'd play Be8. You already see that h8 is undefended, but grabbing it lets White's queen rush back and harass your king. Be8 challenges the bishop on g6 right away, tucks yours safely behind your pawn chain, and keeps the option of rerouting the knight to e7 if needed—all while keeping your king safe. That extra tempo buys you time to activate your rook or push a pawn later.

Takeaway: When your opponent leaves a piece loose, first look for ways to punish the *cause* of the loose piece instead of automatically snapping it off.

I'd play Ne7. You saw the undefended bishop on h8, but grabbing it lets White trade queens and then push a pawn to queen—awkward. Instead, reroute your knight to e7: it eyes the loose bishop on g6, connects your rooks, and still guards your center pawn on d5. The idea is simple—trade off White’s active pieces first, then your king will be safer.

Takeaway: When ahead, favor trading your weaker pieces for their stronger ones before snatching material.

I'd play Rxd5. You spotted the loose bishop on d5—that’s excellent! Instead of trading pawn for bishop right away, use your rook to grab it while also attacking Black’s e5 pawn. This keeps more pieces on the board, which gives you extra chances to create threats and keeps your advantage growing steadily. You already have the rook on the d-file lined up, so the move practically plays itself once you see the loose piece and the tasty pawn behind it.

Takeaway: When you spot a loose enemy piece, ask yourself if you can attack something else at the same time—double trouble is twice as hard for your opponent to handle.

I'd play Qxg4. You saw the chance to grab the bishop, which is great—trading is usually a good idea when your opponent’s piece is loose. But instead of trading a pawn for a bishop, let’s go for something even stronger: snatch their pawn on g4 with the queen. But Black can just slide the queen to h3, and suddenly you’re scrambling instead of improving your position. c4, on the other hand, is simpler and stronger: kick the d5 pawn, which forces Black to deal with it while you’re already making your pawns more active. It also prepares to open lines for your pieces without giving Black a cheap trick like a check or a knight fork.

Takeaway: When you spot a loose pawn, make it move before your opponent can use it to fuel their own counterplay.

I'd play Nf6. Your move, d6, is perfectly fine, but it’s a bit passive—you’re just defending without creating any real threats. Plus, this move often leads to a strong pawn break with d5 later, giving your pieces more space to breathe and letting you fight for the initiative.

Instead, h6 asks the bishop on g5 a direct question: “Where are you going?” If it retreats to e3 or h4, you can follow up with Nh5 or Nh7, trading off White’s strong bishop and freeing your position. The move also gives your king some breathing room on g7, just in case things get sharper. It’s a simple way to start solving your little problems one by one, rather than letting them pile up.

Takeaway: When an opponent’s piece looks annoying, ask it a question—force it to move and reveal its plan.

I'd play Qd6.

Qd6, on the other hand, is active and gives you a clear plan: challenge White’s pawn on f4, which is a bit overextended. This also connects your rooks and keeps an eye on key squares, making your position more coordinated. At your level, it’s great to look for moves that *threaten something* instead of just defending passively.

Takeaway: Always ask, “What does this move actually accomplish?” before playing it.

I'd play h5. I'd play **h6**.

You played a6, which doesn’t really help your position—it just waits, while White can push a4-a5 next to cramp your queenside. h6 immediately challenges the bishop on g5, a strong piece that’s eyeing your kingside and pinning your knight to your queen. I'd play e4.

Trading queens on f1 is fine—your king can handle it, and you’ll have a clear plan of pushing the f-pawn next to roll your kingside pawns forward. The move you played, Qxf7+, gives Black a tempo to castle and smooth development, while e4 keeps the tension and makes Black scramble.

Takeaway: When you can safely challenge the center with a pawn break, do it—it’s often stronger than a flashy check.

I'd play e4. You snapped off the queen — which looks flashy, but Black can just recapture and castle next move, keeping a perfectly playable pawn structure. The real point of the position is that Black is behind in development and still needs to untangle the c8-bishop. Pushing e4 now challenges the d5-pawn, rips open the center before Black can finish developing, and forces him to react while your pieces are already mobile. It’s a clean, principled way to keep the initiative.

Takeaway: When your opponent is behind in development, prefer opening the center over flashy captures.

I'd play e4.

You spotted the queen trade, but trading queens just hands Black easy development and open lines. Playing e4 instead punches through the center, keeping Black’s pieces bottled up—notice how the pawn on d5 is attacked twice and defended only once. If Black plays dxe4, your knight jumps into the hole with Nxe4, eyeing f6 and d6. The whole goal is to make their king unsafe while your pieces get room to breathe.

Takeaway: When you see a pawn that’s attacked twice and defended once, push it and open the position for your pieces.

I'd play Bf4. Your instinct to trade bishops is fine, but Bxg6 right now just hands Black an open h-file for no real benefit—you’re not threatening anything serious yet. Bf4 instead develops a piece with tempo, keeps your strong pawn on e5 defended, and leaves the bishop on g6 looking silly; Black will probably have to weaken their pawns or lose time to kick your pieces, giving you a free hand to build pressure. You want to improve your position first, then trade on your own terms.

Takeaway: Ask “What does my opponent actually fear?” before trading—if the answer is “not much,” keep improving.

I'd play Bf4. The real point isn’t just trading the bishops—it’s getting your pieces to the squares that matter. By putting the bishop on f4 now, you eye f7, keep the e5 pawn safe, and set up a natural sequence: play Bf4 first, then use the d3-bishop to recapture on g6 only if Black lets you. That keeps the tension and gives you chances to build on the light-square play you already started with your knight on e2.

Takeaway: When you have a strong pawn center, develop the piece that defends that center first, then look for the exchange that strengthens your grip.

I'd play Nc4.

Nice job noticing you have pieces to develop, but Bd3 didn’t actually create any threats—your bishop just moved to another safe square. Nc4 is stronger because it jumps to a central square where it attacks the black queen and the d6 pawn at the same time. This forces Black to react right away, and you can follow up by bringing your other pieces into the game while their queen is dancing around.

Takeaway: Look for moves that do more than one useful thing at once, like attacking and defending.

I'd play Nc6. You played castling, which is a fine move, but right now the knight on e5 is sitting in the center looking mighty comfortable—let’s kick it out and take control of the middle of the board. Nc6 attacks the knight, develops your own knight toward the action, and sets you up to challenge White’s d-pawn next. It’s a clear, active plan that beginner players can easily spot and reuse whenever an opponent plants a knight on e5.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a knight happily sitting in the center, look for the simplest way to attack it with one of your pieces.

I'd play Bxe5.

You saw that castling was fine, and that’s great—keeping the king safe is always smart. But right now, White’s knight on e5 eyes important squares, especially f7, and your bishop on d6 is looking right at it. Trading that bishop for the knight removes a key attacker, doubles White’s pawns (which hurts their structure), and lets your queen control the open e-file. It’s a simple but powerful way to fight for the center and free up your position.

Takeaway: When you see an opponent’s strong knight in the center, look for ways to trade it—especially if your bishop is already aiming there.

I'd play Bxe5. The knight on e5 is White’s most active piece, and trading it for your bishop improves your pawn structure by giving you a central pawn duo after dxe5. This also opens the diagonal for your queen to influence e5 and keeps pieces flowing—exactly what you want in a developing position. Your move (castling) was fine, but it missed a chance to grab space and initiative.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s strongest piece is unprotected, look for trades that leave you with better pawns or more active pieces.

I'd play Ngf6.

You played Qe7, which is fine, but it keeps your knight on g8 trapped a little longer. Ngf6 is a much healthier move because it develops that knight right away, eyeing the center squares and even threatening to take the pawn on e4 next turn if White isn’t careful. Putting pieces where they influence the board and fight for space is always a smart plan at this level—it gives you options and keeps your opponent guessing.

Takeaway: When you can develop a new piece and aim it toward the center, do it early—it’s the fastest way to get your army into the game.

I'd play Rab1. You rightly spotted that Black is eyeing your queen on b6, but instead of doubling rooks on the same file, move the other rook to b1 so both rooks guard each other and the queen in one shot—this is called “connecting the rooks,” a fundamental safety move. Once everything is defended, you can calmly push a pawn or trade pieces without worrying about nasty surprises.

Takeaway: When your queen is on an open file, connect your rooks behind it to keep everything safe.

I'd play **Bc4**.

You made a fine developing move with Bd3, but Bc4 teaches a sharper idea: immediately pressuring Black’s knight on d5, which also happens to be eyeing your pawn on d4. By cutting off the knight’s escape square c7 and threatening to swap it off, you force Black to react—usually with …Bf5—while keeping your own pieces flexible. This move also reminds you that knights on advanced outposts are prime targets; snaffling one early can simplify the game in your favor.

Takeaway: When your opponent parks a knight on an outpost, ask which of your minor pieces can most cheaply attack it—often the answer leads to a strong, forcing reply.

I'd play Ngf6.

Your move Qe7 is fine, but Ngf6 is even better—it develops a piece with tempo by attacking e4 while also freeing the knight on d7. The pawn on e4 is a juicy target, and hitting it twice (with your knight and bishop) forces White to react, giving you time to improve your position. Plus, Ngf6 keeps your king safer by preparing to castle kingside more smoothly.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a weak pawn, look for ways to attack it *while developing*—double-purpose moves are the hallmark of strong play.

I'd play Bc3. The student played Rfb1, which keeps things solid but misses a chance to grab the initiative—Black’s pawns on e5 and g5 are floating, and your bishop on d5 already eyes the knight on g8. Bc3 flexes that bishop to a strong diagonal where it attacks e5 right away and keeps an eye on both sides of the board, making Black choose between defending the pawn or trading minor pieces in a way that opens lines for your rooks. It’s a simple move that turns a quiet position into one with active pressure, something intermediates often overlook when they default to passive rook moves.

Takeaway: In calm positions, ask which piece can step forward to create new threats before piling on defense.

I'd play Bc3. You chose a solid move, but the real opportunity here is to use the bishop’s power to challenge Black’s center and open lines for your rooks. Bc3 immediately targets the e5 pawn, which is Black’s main source of activity, and prepares to trade bishops—simplifying the position while keeping your own pressure. The key idea is to provoke a weakness (like ...Bf6, blocking the rook) or force Black to retreat, making it easier to double rooks on the b-file later.

Takeaway: When you have a bishop aiming at the opponent’s pawn chain, look for ways to target the base (here, e5) to disrupt their structure and create long-term pressure.

I'd play Ngf6. You're right to focus on piece activity, but Qe7 just lets White sidestep with g3 without any real gain. Ngf6 puts immediate pressure on e4, opens the diagonal for your light-squared bishop, and prepares to castle—all while keeping the tension in the center. It’s the kind of flexible, centralizing move that fits a classic "develop with threats" plan at your level.

Takeaway: When developing, ask which move both completes a piece’s development *and* challenges your opponent’s setup.

I'd play Bf4. You saw that Black is eyeing your pawn on e5, so you wanted to kick the knight away with Ne2—but Bf4 does the same job even better! It defends e5 and adds a strong piece to the fight while keeping your other pieces flexible. I'd play Bf4.

Bf4 does a better job of *protecting* that pawn while keeping pressure on the dark squares around Black’s king. I'd play **Qd8**.

I'd play Bf4. The move you played, Ne2, blocks your bishop on g2, which is one of your strongest pieces—it’s doing great work on the long diagonal and shouldn’t be shut in. I'd play Qd7.

It’s about stepping into the initiative instead of just responding.

Takeaway: When you spot a piece that’s overextended, look for ways to attack it while improving your own position at the same time.

I'd play g6. I'd play Bf7. You just blundered your pawn on e5 by leaving it hanging—Qd6 didn’t protect it at all. I'd play Bg5. You spotted the loose knight on g4, but pushing c6 doesn’t help—White can just snap the knight while you’re busy on the queenside. I'd play Bg5. You noticed your knight on g4 is hanging—that’s great awareness! I'd play a4. I'd play Nc4. I'd play d4. I'd play d4. You tried Qg3, which eyes the h-pawn, but Black can just push it two squares and the queen has to move again. With d4 you strike at the center immediately: you attack Black’s e5 pawn and open the diagonal for your bishop, aiming straight at the king. I'd play d4. You wanted to stir up kingside trouble with Qg3, but Black simply tucks the knight back to g6 and White has no real threats—your queen is misplaced. I'd play Ng6. I'd play Bb5. Your Bc4 is fine, but Bb5 is even clearer: it develops your bishop with a little bite by attacking Black’s knight on c6. I'd play **a4**.

Your instinct to jump into tactics with Nd5 isn’t wrong—it’s proactive and sharp—but here, Black’s position is solid and the knight on d5 can be traded or retreated without real damage. Plus, it sets up ideas like Qc2 to eye c5 or preparing b4 to challenge Black’s pawn chain.

I'd play Ng6.

That rook can’t easily run away, so you’re gaining time while keeping all your other pieces ready to jump in. The idea is simple: trade off your knight for the bishop on f8, leaving Black with a weak king-side pawn structure and a passive rook.

Takeaway: Look for the first move that both develops a piece AND creates an immediate, visible problem your opponent must solve.

I'd play Re8. You already have a strong central pawn on c4, so there’s no rush to push another one—let’s first shore up our coordination. Re8 both eyeballs White’s advanced e5 pawn and keeps our rooks connected, which is a rock-solid way to prepare for any central tension. Takeaway: When you already have a pawn controlling the center, use your other pieces to add pressure before committing to another pawn break.

I'd play Be4. This pressures Black right away, gives your pieces more room, and keeps the board lively without giving up anything.

Takeaway: When an opponent attacks a piece, look for *your* way to create an even stronger threat instead of just defending.

I'd play Re8. You played d5, which is a natural attempt to kick the bishop away, but right now the bishop isn’t doing much—it’s just staring at your pawn on f7. Instead, Re8 puts a rook on an open file, challenges White’s pawn on e5, and keeps everything defended. That pawn on e5 is a target, and your rook can help pile up on it later while staying safe.

I'd play Re8. However, d5 walks into a small trick: after exd6 Qxd6 White’s queen naturally eyes b4, which can lead to awkward queen trades or awkward knight moves on the edge of the board. I'd play Bxf6.

I'd play Be4. I'd play Be4.

I'd play Bxf6.

I'd play Bg5.

I'd play Be3. You tossed the knight straight at Black’s kingside, which feels aggressive but actually lets Black push …c5 next move and start their own counterplay in the center. I'd play Bg5.

I'd play Be3. You played Ng5, which chases the bishop, but Black can just move it and your knight ends up a bit loose—better to keep improving your position first. I'd play Nc6. You picked Nf6 to kick the e4-pawn, which is active, but the knight cuts off the queen’s view and leaves a7 hanging—after White plays Qb3 it actually puts *your* queen on the spot. Qh5 snaps onto the weak pawn on h7 while defending the knight, creating threats that force Black to scramble instead of calmly improving their position. Takeaway: When a piece is undefended, defend it *with a threat*—not just by retreating silently.

I'd play Rf7. You saw the knight on g5 is hanging—good—but you retreated the rook to f1, which just hides without a plan. Rf7 instead plants the rook on g7’s doorstep, threatening to snap the pawn and expose Black’s king. Black has to scramble (maybe Ne5 or h6), and every move they make loosens something else. You stay aggressive while keeping all your pieces safe.

Takeaway: When you spot an undefended piece, ask “Can I attack something even bigger instead of just defending?”

I'd play O-O. Castling is almost always the right idea early in the game—it tucks your king away safely and connects your rooks, giving your pieces room to work together. Since you were already thinking of castling, you’re on the right track; the only “mistake” was not doing it sooner! Now Black has to deal with your active pieces, especially the bishop on d3 pointing at their king.

Takeaway: Castle early unless you see a clear reason not to.

I'd play Nf7. You spotted that the knight on g5 is attacked, but instead of just pulling the rook back you can send the knight *forward* to do real work—it lands on f7, attacking the pawn on d6, the queen on d8, and the rook on h8 all at once. The instant threat is Ng5–f7, and if Black isn’t careful the knight stays there as a hero, cutting off the king’s escape and setting up tricks like Bxd4+ followed by queen recaptures. It turns your “oops, my piece is under fire” moment into a chance to stir up real confusion.

Takeaway: When a piece is attacked, first ask “can it jump somewhere even stronger?” before you retreat.

I'd play Bd7. You played a6 to kick my bishop away, but the real job is getting your pieces out—Bd7 does that AND protects your knight on c6. Now White has to move the bishop anyway, and you’re ready to castle next, which keeps your king safe. The big idea is “develop a piece and connect your rooks”; Bd7 does both while staying flexible.

Takeaway: Instead of pushing a pawn, ask “which piece hasn’t moved yet?”—move that first.

I'd play Bxd2+.

You saw how useful it is to castle early—great job! But right now, White’s knight on d2 is attacking your bishop, and it’s also one of the few piecesWhite has developed. Trading it off with Bxd2+ not only removes that knight but also forces White’s queen to recapture, which slows down their plans. It’s a simple way to keep the game balanced while making White work to catch up.

Takeaway: When your opponent develops a piece that’s attacking yours, look for ways to trade it off—it can disrupt their whole setup.

I'd play Ne5.

You chose O-O, which is safe, but it lets Black regroup easily with moves like Nh5 or Re8. Ne5 grabs space in the center and creates tension—it eyes f7 and c6, nudging Black’s pieces into awkward spots while keeping your own flexible. At this level, mastering when to improve pieces instead of rushing development is key, and Ne5 keeps the position alive with clear targets. The idea isn’t to win instantly but to build pressure that forces Black to make concessions later.

Takeaway: When development feels “done,” look for knights or bishops that can jump to squares where they threaten something concrete.

I'd play Bd7. You spotted that White’s bishop is loose, but kicking it with a6 just lets it retreat to d3 where it eyes your weak pawns on h7 and e6—exactly the ones you’re trying to protect. Bd7 instead develops a piece, connects the rooks, and keeps the option to castle short next move while still keeping an eye on that bishop. It also sets up a neat trick: if White ever plays Bxc6, you recapture b7xc6 doubling your pawns but opening the b-file for your rook, turning a static weakness into dynamic play.

Takeaway: When a piece is loose, first ask which of your developed pieces can attack it without creating new weaknesses—often the simplest developing move is the strongest.

I'd play O-O. Castling is the strongest move here—not just to safety, but to finish your development and connect your rooks. The student played a6, which nudges the bishop but doesn’t address the core issue: your king is stuck in the center while White has minor pieces ready to jump. Once you castle, you can calmly develop your light-squared bishop to d7 and start eyeing the e5 pawn or pushing your queenside pawns.

Takeaway: When your king is still in the center in an open or semi-open position, castling should be your first priority—even before pawn moves.

I'd play Ne5.

You missed this because you were thinking “safety first,” yet the real safety comes from active play that forces Black to react. By jumping to e5, you create immediate tension and open lines for your other pieces without risking a thing.

Takeaway: At this level, always ask “which piece can jump into the most squares?” before making a quiet move like castling or pawns to the side.

I'd play Qg5. You correctly spotted that castling is safe, but the best moves often ask a question—here, Qg5 directly targets White’s loose pawn on e3 and the pawn on g2, which now needs defending. Takeaway: When your opponent’s structure has loose pawns, look for active piece play that highlights them—even if you don’t win material right away.

I'd play Bxf4.

I'd play O-O. I'd play Bxf4. I'd play Bxf6. You chose to push your pawn, but here’s a golden chance to trade bishop for knight—Black’s knight is guarding the important pawn on e5, and after it’s gone that pawn will hang. I'd play Bxf6. You saw the center pawn push e4, but right now Black’s knight on f6 is a nagging piece—it eyes d5, supports e4, and keeps your dark-squared bishop out of play. I'd play Nd2. You played e4 to block the bishop, but Black simply pushes ...g5 and the bishop slips out anyway—plus you’ll have to recapture with your knight and lose time. Takeaway: When your opponent’s bishop eyes two pawns that share a color, re-route a piece to protect both instead of pushing a pawn that just creates new holes.

I'd play Rad8. You're right to want to bring your rook somewhere useful, but the one on *f8* already guards the knight—letting the rook on *a8* sit idle is like choosing to play with half your army! I'd play Nbd2. Your move Qxd8 is totally fine—it trades queens and keeps things simple—but there’s a nicer way to play here. I'd play **Rad8**.

You already found a solid move by activating one rook, but bringing the *other* rook to the same open file is a classic way to double your power. Doubling rooks on the d-file keeps everything defended while making it harder for White to untangle, and it sets you up to slide the queen over to the queenside later.

Takeaway: When a file is open, stacking both rooks behind a key piece (here, the knight on d5) often wins the fight for control.

I'd play e5. I'd play Bxc6.

The idea is simple: trade a bishop for a knight and leave Black with a pawn that can never defend itself perfectly. It also keeps the light-square bishop alive for future play, which can put pressure on Black’s position.

Takeaway: A small pawn weakness is easier to target than a queen trade that leaves all of Black’s pieces on the board.

I'd play Bxc6.

In this Ruy Lopez structure you’ve got a solid bind on the light squares, and Black’s knight on c6 is the key defender of e5. Swapping it off loosens Black’s pawns (b7 drops next), gives you the bishop-pair, and leaves Black with no active plan—he’ll just have to sit while you pick your next target. You played the safe queen trade, which keeps things level but hands Black immediate equality; Bxc6 keeps real winning chances alive by fixing a long-term weakness.

Takeaway: When you have a lead in development and the opponent’s knight is the glue holding things together, trading it can be stronger than grabbing the queen.

I'd play Nd5. I'd play Nf4. You spotted a cool pawn push with e4, but it actually walks into a little trick—White can check your king with the bishop and cause a fuss. I'd play Nf4. I'd play Nd5.

I'd play Nd5.

I'd play Bxg6.

I'd play Nf4. You had the right idea with e4—trying to strike at the center—but it allows White to harass your queen with Bc4+. I'd play **Bf4**. You castled, and that’s fine—you get your king to safety. But right now Black’s bishop on d6 eyes your weak pawn on e3 and can jump into your position with …Bh2 tricks.
