I'd play **a6**.

Your move, Nb6, isn’t wrong—it just rushes the point. White’s bishop on c4 is awkward, but kicking it right away lets White tuck it to d3 and resume development without any real harm. **a6** tells that bishop, “Decide now,” while giving your own knight a clear outpost on b6 later. It also prevents any annoying b5 ideas from White and buys you a tempo if they move the queen again. The plan is simple: make White waste a move on the bishop, then reroute your pieces to where they truly belong.

Takeaway: When an opponent’s piece is loose but not hanging, create a small threat that forces them to spend a tempo before you commit yours.

I'd play Bxf6.

You saw your pieces were floating in the middle and wanted to tidy up with Re1, but the real target is Black’s king. Bxf6 cracks open the pawn shield: take the knight, and if Black recaptures with the bishop you double on the f-file; if they recapture with a pawn you get a powerful knight on e4 aimed at d6 and f6. It’s the kind of trade that makes Black’s position suddenly uncomfortable rather than just “equal.”

Takeaway: When your opponent’s king is still castled, look for the bishop or knight that can swap itself for the pawn in front of him—opening lines often wins more than tidying your own ranks.

I'd play Nf5. You spotted that the e7-bishop is the only piece defending Black’s king, but Re1 didn’t actually challenge it; Nf5 lands right on its doorstep, forcing the bishop to move while also eyeing g7 and creating the threat of a discovered check if Black moves the queen. If Black tries to trade queens first, you’ll just recapture with the rook, reinforcing the center and keeping all the pressure. This move turns your knights into active aggressors instead of passive defenders—exactly the kind of central outpost play you’ll want to master at this level.

Takeaway: When you see an enemy piece defending a key square or the king, look for a way to attack it directly with a minor piece instead of relying on a rook or queen.

I'd play cxd5.

You played O-O, which is fine, but trading in the center is even better. Right now White has a strong pawn center with pawns on c4 and d5; by capturing on d5 you force White to recapture, which either makes their pawns weaker or opens the position for your pieces. After cxd5 White will likely take back with the c-pawn, and then you can develop naturally with moves like O-O and Nd7, keeping everything safe and active.

Takeaway: When you see an opponent pushing pawns in the center, look for chances to trade them and ease the pressure.

I'd play cxd5. You chose to castle right away, which keeps the king safe but misses a key chance to strike in the center. Capturing on d5 opens the c-file, lets your pieces breathe, and gives White a pawn weakness to target later—this is how you take control of the position. You already know how to spot hanging pawns, and here Black can actually *force* one on c4 by recapturing with the c-pawn, so go for it.

Takeaway: In similar structures, always check whether you can capture toward the center to create or attack pawn weaknesses.

I'd play Nd7. That knight is a bit tangled on b8—it doesn’t really protect anything useful. Moving it to d7 gets it off the back rank, eyes the juicy e5 square, and keeps an eye on both White’s knights at once; the pawn on b6 gets extra backup too. You played Nc6, which does attack the knight on d4, but pushing the knight further forward often just gives the opponent more targets—Qd5 was becoming awkward for you later.

Takeaway: Developing a piece toward the center and adding defense to existing pawns often solves two problems at once.

I'd play Ba4.

You spotted the undefended knight on c6—but instead of trading right away, ask yourself: “Can I keep my good piece?” Moving the bishop to a4 keeps it safe, still eyes the knight, and spots Black’s own loose bishop on g4. If Black swaps bishops next move, you’ll recapture with the knight and end up with two pieces trained on c6: that’s a double attack you can practice again and again.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended piece, first look for a safe way to keep your own piece on the board while still hitting the target.

I'd play Ba4.

You spotted the loose bishop on b5 and tried to win material, but Bxc6+ just forces Black to recapture with the pawn and opens the b-file for their rook, giving them counterplay. Ba4 keeps the pressure on the knight on c6 while also defending your own pawn on c2—this move doesn’t rush, it strengthens your position first. The real plan is to trade off Black’s best piece (the bishop on g4) with h3 next, leaving their position cramped and your pieces dominant. You’ll frequently see this pattern: *defend first, then attack*—it’s safer and keeps your structure solid.

Takeaway: When you have a loose piece under attack, consider retreating it to safety while also improving your position—don’t just grab material at the cost of your own harmony.

I'd play Ne5.

Your move Bxc6+ is fine, but Ne5 is sharper and teaches a key idea: when you have a knight that can jump into the enemy position, look for the weakest spot—here, the pinned knight on c6 *and* the pawn on f7. By planting the knight on e5, you keep Black busy defending both threats while also eyeing the bishop on g4. This move keeps more pieces on the board and keeps the pressure on, which is exactly what you want when you're a bit ahead in development.

Takeaway: When your knight can reach a central outpost that attacks multiple targets, prioritize it over routine exchanges—your opponent will have to waste moves defending instead of developing.

I'd play Ng5.

You chose Qd2, which isn't bad—it keeps things safe—but Ng5 is stronger because it creates a real threat. The knight jumps to g5, attacking the pawn on e6 and also eyeing f7 and h7, which forces Black to react. This isn’t just shuffling; you’re making Black deal with pressure while you set up a potential follow-up like swapping off the bishop on h5 for your knight, which would weaken Black’s pawns. Instead of moving the queen again, look for moves that ask your opponent a question.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s pieces look cozy, try to nudge them with a direct threat—it often leads to better chances than waiting.

I'd play Ng5. You wanted to spot threats and keep the position fluid, but Qd2 just tucked the queen behind the bishop with no clear follow-up. Ng5 immediately targets e6 and f7—two weak pawns that Black must defend—and your knight can’t be kicked by a pawn. It also clears the way for ideas like Bd3 and h4-h5 later, giving you a concrete plan instead of just shuffling pieces.

Takeaway: When you see two undefended pawns on the same file, look for a single move that attacks both.

I'd play Ng5. You played Qd2, which blocks the bishop on e3 and does nothing active—the queen had no business going there. Ng5 hits three pawns at once while keeping the h3 pawn safe, and Black can’t defend everything. If they play Bg6, your bishop can jump to d3, putting pressure on the kingside and making Black regret playing their bishop to h5.

Takeaway: When your opponent leaves pawns loose, always ask which knight jump creates the most threats before moving anything else.

I'd play h4.

You played Kb1, which is safe, but it misses a great chance to strike. Black's pawn on g5 is a little overextended—it has moved far but isn't well defended. Pushing h4 creates immediate tension: if Black takes (gxh4), you can recapture with your pawn, opening the h-file for your rook, and suddenly Black's king is feeling the heat. If Black doesn't take, you've gained space on the kingside, and your pieces are ready to jump into action there.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s pawn is far forward and not well defended, look for ways to challenge it with your own pawns—it can open lines and create attacking chances.

I'd play f4.

You played Kb1, which is perfectly safe, but it misses a great chance to challenge Black’s strong pawn center. Right now Black’s pawns on e5 and g5 cramp your position and give their pieces lots of space. Playing f4 immediately fights back—it kicks the pawn on e5, opens the diagonal for your bishop on g2, and can even create a weakness on g5 later. The idea is simple: start undermining their center so your pieces can breathe and your rooks can line up behind open files.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong pawn center, look for pawn breaks that challenge it before retreating or defending.

I'd play h4.

You played Kb1, which is safe but misses a golden opportunity to seize space and provoke weaknesses. Right now Black’s kingside pawns are advanced and ripe for challenge—pushing h4 immediately asks them how they plan to handle the soft spot on g5. If they take, your rook can swing over to f1, eyeing the newly exposed file; if they duck, you gain a target to attack later. The move is aggressive yet principled: when you enjoy space and activity, open lines and fix pawns before they can retreat.

Takeaway: In open positions, look first for pawn breaks that create or expose enemy weaknesses before defaulting to slow king moves.

I'd play Nd7.

You spotted the “free” knight on e5, but grabbing it with the bishop costs you more than it’s worth: every trade should develop *your* game too. Nd7 kicks the knight *and* brings your own knight toward the action, eyeing b6 or f6 next—simple, active, and reusable. You’ll feel the difference when your pieces start helping each other instead of just taking whatever’s hanging.

Takeaway: Always ask “does this move also improve my position?” before capturing.

I'd play Bd3.

You played Bc4, which actually gives Black a little check on e8 that forces you to move your knight instead of making progress. Bd3 does something much simpler: it brings the bishop out where it eyes the weak pawn on g6 and keeps your king safe by castling next move. The pawn on g6 can’t run away, so you’re winning it or getting a big positional edge without any tricks.

Takeaway: When you develop, first look for squares that attack something or keep your king safe—don’t just drop pieces on random good squares.

I'd play Nd7. The knight on e5 is a monster, and trading it right away feels natural, but look how flexible Nd7 is: it kicks the knight, develops your own knight toward the center, and keeps your pieces coordinated. The student’s move gave White a free tempo to recapture and open the d-file; Nd7 instead turns the tables—after Nxd7 you get to recapture with the bishop, keeping tension and aiming at h2.

Takeaway: When a strong enemy piece lands in your camp, ask first if you can attack it with development rather than swap it immediately.

I'd play Qg5. You spotted the loose knight on e5—but taking it lets White recapture and claim the center, while the queen sortie to g5 does more: it attacks not only the knight but also the soft pawn on e3 and the bishop that guards the king. The idea isn’t just pressure; it forces White to spend a move defending (say, f4) while you swing the queen to h4 and keep the kingside loose, buying time to jump a knight to c6 or even castle long. You traded the initiative for a pawn; Qg5 keeps the tension where it belongs.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s pieces sit on the fifth rank, look for queen moves that attack several of them at once—they often create the sort of chaos you want before you commit to exchanging.

I'd play Be2. You played Bc4, which looks active, but it gives Black a free tempo with Re8+, allowing them to get organized while you scramble your pieces. Be2 does the same job—developing a piece—but quietly, keeping the tension and letting you calmly prepare to castle or reroute the bishop later. The real target here is Black’s awkward queen on d8, which wants to recapture on d6 but can’t yet without dropping the c5 pawn.

Takeaway: When development feels “obvious,” check if the obvious move creates new problems—often the quieter piece fits better into the long game.

I'd play **Nbd2**.

You played Re1, which is fine, but right now your biggest priority is *developing your pieces*. The knight on b1 hasn’t moved yet, and Nbd2 gives it a clear job: guarding the key e4 pawn while opening the path for your other pieces to get into the game. It also keeps your structure solid and prepares to bring your queen or other rook into play later. Re1 didn’t hurt you, but Nbd2 does more to make your position stronger.

Takeaway: Always ask which of your pieces haven’t moved yet—getting them out early builds coordination and keeps your game flexible.

I'd play Bd3.

You spotted the hanging pawn on d6, which is great—keeping an eye on undefended pieces is a habit strong players share. But Bc4 misses a stronger idea: attacking g6 with the bishop puts *real* pressure on Black, since that pawn is already under strain from your knight on f3. Bd3 also develops naturally while threatening to trade bishops and weaken Black’s kingside structure, which ties into a common theme at your level: aim at pawns that aren’t defended by other pawns, especially near the king. You’ll often find these “hooks” lead to weakening moves like ...h6 or ...g5 that make your opponent’s position harder to handle.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended pawn, look for ways to pile on rather than just grab it—extra attackers create real problems for your opponent.

I'd play Nc6. You played cxd4, which sounds logical because it wins a pawn, but after White takes back with the queen, your queen on a5 is suddenly loose and can be chased around. Nc6 instead adds protection to your queen and puts pressure on the central pawn on d4—exactly the kind of support your queen needed. It also eyes e5, keeping White guessing while you finish developing your minor pieces.

Takeaway: Always ask “what’s my most vulnerable piece?” before grabbing a pawn; defending it first is usually safer than fixing it later.

I'd play Nbd2. You chose Re1, which is solid but passive—it just waits. Nbd2 develops a piece, supports the e4 pawn, and keeps plans open: you can follow up with Qe2, connecting the rooks, or reroute the knight to f1-e3 if Black challenges the center. It also avoids giving Black any targets, like the loose bishop on d3 after Bf4. At your level, activating every piece with clear ideas is how you build pressure.

Takeaway: In quiet positions, developing with a purpose beats just defending—ask what each new move *adds* to your position.

I'd play Bf4.

You played Re1, which is fine—it keeps everything protected—but a little passive. Bf4 is a great way to develop with a little bite: your bishop eyes the d6 pawn, and if Black isn’t careful you can push e5, cracking open the center while they’re still getting organized. It also stays flexible: you’re not committing to a big pawn push yet, just improving a piece and watching for Black’s reaction.

Takeaway: When you can develop with a small threat, it often steers the game in your direction without over-extending.

I'd play c4. You opened the center too soon with cxd4, letting White's pieces pour in. Instead, hit back with c4—it chases the queen away from b3 and asks White how they plan to keep their center together while their queen is running. Once the queen moves, you can bring your knight to c6 to add more pressure right where White is weakest.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s queen is just outside your pawn chain, look for a pawn push to dislodge her before she teams up with the rest of their pieces.

I'd play Nc6. You played cxd4, which opens the center too early—White’s pieces spring to life and your queen on a5 gets chased around. Nc6 instead builds firm pressure on d4 while reinforcing both your queen and rook; once the knight lands, you can follow with b5 or even reroute the other knight via c8 to d6 to clamp down on that pawn. It’s a quiet move that actually tightens your whole position and keeps the initiative.

Takeaway: When you have pieces lined up on a key square, reinforce them before capturing.

I'd play Qb3. You played a solid move with castling, which is fine, but Qb3 fixes a key beginner mistake: ignoring your opponent’s weak pawns. Right now Black’s b7 pawn is undefended, and Qb3 puts pressure on it while also eyeing the d5 pawn—just like grabbing a free snack that’s been left out! By targeting these weak pawns, you force Black to waste time defending them, giving you space to develop the rest of your pieces quickly.

Takeaway: Always look for undefended pawns or pieces—pressuring them early can win you easy time and space.

I'd play Bd6. You’re a move away from castling, and the best way to smooth that path is to kick White’s queen off the open central file—plus you’re trading a flank pawn move for a developing move that lands on your most active diagonal. The fact that it also protects your rook on f8 and your pawn on c7 is a bonus; you’re essentially fixing two potential weaknesses while activating your bishop. Your move h6 pushed the enemy piece back, but it didn’t improve your position as much as Bd6 does, because it didn’t bring a new piece into the game.

Takeaway: When you can develop a piece while also attacking a strong enemy piece, that’s usually worth a small delay in castling.

I'd play Bd6. You spotted the hanging bishop on g5, but trading pieces when you're slightly behind in development can help your opponent more than you—notice how after h6 Be3, their bishop is still active and now eyes d4 or f4. Instead, Bd6 challenges the powerful queen while activating your bishop toward the center and kingside, and it keeps the pressure on e5 after Bxf6 gxf6, disrupting White’s pawn structure. This move also nudges White toward trading queens on d6, which usually eases Black’s play in middlegames with symmetrical pawns.

Takeaway: When ahead in development, look to trade pieces—but when behind, trade pieces *only* if it worsens your opponent’s structure or eases your cramped position.

I'd play Bd6. Your move h6 chases the bishop, but it doesn’t actually win anything—White just retreats, and you’ve weakened your king’s position for no real gain. Bd6 is much stronger: you attack White’s queen directly, forcing her to move, and at the same time you improve your own bishop by putting it on a nice open diagonal. If White tries to exchange queens, you recapture with your bishop and suddenly you’re trading a passive piece for an active one, which is a great deal for you.

Takeaway: Before making a move that chases a piece, ask: “What do I actually win, and what does it cost me?”

I'd play Qb3. You missed a chance to put immediate pressure on Black’s weak pawns while keeping your own position solid—Qb3 hits the b7 and d5 pawns in one move, and Black has to spend a tempo defending instead of developing smoothly. The queen also guards your b2 pawn, so your rook on c1 stays safe, and you’re ready to meet …Qa5+ with Kf1 without losing material. This kind of active queen move is a hallmark of stronger play: you’re not just casting out of habit, you’re seizing the moment to dictate the game.

Takeaway: Before castling, ask if the queen can strike at weaknesses right now—often the answer is yes.

I'd play Qb3.

Castling is perfectly fine, but it misses a great chance to turn up the heat right away. Black’s knight on d7 is hanging if you threaten the pawn on b7, and your queen slices through the middle of the board, giving you control over dark squares and keeping Black guessing—will you double on the b-file, trade bishops on h3, or jump the knight to g4 later? This move punishes Black’s slightly loose pawns while keeping your pieces flexible and dangerous, which is exactly how you want to play when you already have a strong center and active pieces.

Takeaway: When your pieces are developed and your pawns control the center, look for queen moves that attack weak pawns and open lines for your other pieces.

I'd play Nd5. You noticed the knight was loose, but moving it to e2 just shuffles it around without punching back—Nd5 instead lands right where Black’s knight is sitting, threatening to kick it away and grab a free pawn next move. It also clears the back rank so your rooks can start working together down the open files. That combo of attack and space is exactly what beginners should train themselves to spot.

Takeaway: Always ask “where can my pieces annoy the most targets at once?” before retreating.

I'd play Nd5.

Instead of retreating to a quiet square, this move hits two big targets at once—Black’s knight and the e7 pawn. If Black responds with Nd7, you get a clear plan: push f5 to open the long diagonal for your bishop while keeping the pressure. It’s a stronger way to fight for the dark squares than just defending f4, and it fits with the theme of using your knight actively when it’s under fire.

Takeaway: When a piece is attacked, look for a counter-attack that creates new threats—not just safe retreats.

I'd play Nd5. The knight leap does two powerful things at once: it kicks the black knight away from the center and creates real pressure on the e7 pawn, which can’t easily be defended. Your move, Ne2, was perfectly fine, but Nd5 actually *transforms* the position—Black has to scramble (maybe Nd7 or e5), and you’ll follow up with f5 to open the position for your bishop pair. That kind of double threat is exactly how you convert a small edge into a real initiative at this level.

Takeaway: When you have a central knight, look for the square where it attacks the most undefended targets—those are the jumps that turn “nothing” into a plan.

I'd play Nd4.

You saw the threat on the knight, but the real action is in the center and queenside. By plunging the knight into d4, you attack White’s weak pawn on b5 and disrupt their knight on f3, which is eyeing your kingside. This forces White to react immediately—either trade knights or retreat—which gives you time to activate your pieces and strike back with ...exd4 or ...c6 later. You missed a chance to seize control and make their position uncomfortable instead of just defending.

Takeaway: When your piece is attacked, ask: “Where can I land that creates *my* threats, not just saves the piece?”

I'd play Nd4.

You saw the knight on d5 sitting pretty, but instead of backing away, challenge it head-on. Moving your knight to d4 attacks the pawn on b5 (a little bonus) and kicks White’s strong knight before it can cause more trouble. It also keeps your pieces active and gives you room to develop. You missed a great chance to stir up the position—next time, look for spots where you can trade or annoy your opponent’s best piece.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong knight in the center, look for ways to attack it right away instead of stepping back.

I'd play **Nh5**.

You noticed the pawn on f6 is weak—great instinct! Instead of trading on f6, let’s *punish* Black for leaving it unprotected. Jumping the knight to h5 attacks f6 again while also eyeing the pawn on g7 (a double attack). If Black takes the pawn on e5, our knight recaptures and lands right on the outpost you just created. This keeps piece activity high and teaches you how knights thrive on the edge when they target loose pawns.

Takeaway: When an opponent leaves a pawn undefended, look for pieces that can attack it twice rather than automatically trading.

I'd play Bc8. You noticed that the pawn on d7 needs help—good eye!—but instead of moving your queen (which is your strongest piece), you can use a less valuable piece to defend it. The bishop on b7 isn’t doing much right now, so bringing it to c8 keeps everything safe while keeping your queen ready for bigger plans. It also sets up a discovery if White ever tries to grab that pawn.

Takeaway: When a pawn needs defending, first check if a minor piece can do the job without tying down your queen.

I'd play Nd4.

You played Nce7, which was fine, but it didn’t really *do* much—just shuffled the knight to a slightly safer square. Nd4 is exciting: you jab the knight right into the center, attacking White’s pawn on b5 and annoying the knight on f3. After White takes your knight, your pawn on e5 takes back and suddenly you’ve traded a knight for a strong central pawn, opening lines for your rooks and bishop. It turns a quiet, slightly passive position into one where you’re calling the shots.

Takeaway: In semi-open positions, sending a knight to a central outpost often gives you active play and forces trades that work in your favor.

I'd play Nh5. You already spotted that the black pawn on f6 is loose, but taking it right away lets Black recapture and comfortably finish development. Nh5 forces Black to deal with the knight—either kick it back with …g6, weakening the dark squares, or take on e5 right away, handing you the strong outpost on e5. It also keeps pressure on g7, so Black can’t just ignore the threat.

Takeaway: When you see a loose pawn, look for ways to target it with multiple pieces before you actually take it.

I'd play Bc8. You played Qd8, which is perfectly safe, but it just shuffles the queen around without really improving your position. Bc8 does a couple of great things: it defends the weak pawn on d7 in a way that also tucks the bishop to a safer square, making it harder for White to target. Plus, moving the bishop opens the rook on h8 to join the action later, like doubling on the h-file if White leaves the pawn on h2 hanging.

Takeaway: Defend weak pawns with developing moves that also keep your pieces active and connected.

I'd play Nh5. You spotted the e5 pawn hanging and acted quickly—great instinct—but grabbing it right away lets Black recapture smoothly and keeps their kingside solid. Nh5 instead pressures both f6 and g7, forcing Black to cough up the e5 pawn anyway while their pawn shield starts to spring leaks. If they take on e5 you leap in with the other knight, and if they push f5 you just keep rattling the door on g7.

Takeaway: When an opponent’s pawn shield looks stretched, don’t just snatch the hanging pawn—look for the move that weakens it further.

I'd play Bd3.

You chose e4, which is fine, but Bd3 does two important things at once: it chases the black bishop away while also protecting your queen on c2. That little extra safety lets you start building a plan—maybe push c4 next to grab space, or slide the rook to c1 to connect everything. It’s a simple trade: you attack their bishop, they move it, and now your queen has one less worry.

Takeaway: When your queen is loose, look for ways to both defend it *and* do something active with the same move.

I'd play Bd3. You spotted that Black’s bishop on f5 is loose—attacking it is exactly the right idea! Instead of pushing the pawn, simply move your own bishop to d3, where it also shields your queen on c2. This keeps everything protected while stepping up the pressure.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended piece, look first for a safe way to attack it that also guards your own loose pieces.

I'd play Bc8. You spotted that the pawn on d7 could be in trouble, but Qd8 just tucks the queen away without doing anything active—Black’s pieces are already shoving each other around in the bottom right. Bc8 shores up d7 *while* getting the bishop off the crowded back rank so the rooks can connect and swing over to the kingside where White’s pawns look loose. With g3 only defended by the bishop on e3, Black soon gets ideas like doubling rooks on the h-file or pushing …g5 under good circumstances.

Takeaway: When you firm up a weak pawn, pick the defender that also unclogs a traffic jam and points toward the opponent’s soft spots.

I'd play Qd6. You noticed the pressure on d3, but moving the king to d8 just hides—it doesn’t fight for the light squares. Sliding the queen to d6 keeps everything defended (the bishop on d7 and the pawn on c6) while hitting that weak d3 pawn squarely; if White pushes d4, you snap it off with Qxd4 and suddenly your pieces breathe freely. You’re also eyeing a future ...Be6 or ...c5, activating your rooks without ever giving White a target.

Takeaway: When you spot a loose pawn, look first for a way to attack it with a piece that’s already doing something useful.

I'd play Bd3. You're absolutely right that Black's bishop on f5 is a strong piece, but moving the pawn to e4 just lets them trade and improve their structure (e.g., Be6, then maybe ...e4 later). By stepping in with Bd3, you not only attack the bishop but also clear the path for your rook to swing over to d1 or h3—plus, you instantly shore up your queen. If they retreat, you've gained space; if they trade, you've activated your bishop for a potential kingside push.

Takeaway: When an opponent’s piece is loose, ask whether a piece attack or a pawn push fits your broader plan—often the piece move does more than just harass.

I'd play Be8. You spotted that the bishop on g6 is doing nothing to help White, but moving the king to d8 just shuffles without a clear plan. Be8 attacks that idle bishop right away and swings the piece toward the center, where it can help defend your pawn on c6 or eye the pawn on d3—both squares you already care about. It also keeps your king safe where it is, which is usually smarter than walking it out early.

Takeaway: When you see an opponent’s piece that’s not defended by anything else, look for ways to attack it and improve your own piece in the same move.

I'd play O-O.

Instead, castling is the simplest and safest move here: it tucks your king away behind a solid pawn shield and connects your rooks so they can start working together. At this level, getting your king safe early should almost always be your first priority unless there’s a clear reason not to.

Takeaway: Castle early to keep your king safe and link your rooks—it’s almost never wrong when you can.

I'd play **Nf6**.

Instead, Nf6 develops a piece, targets that loose pawn on h5, and keeps everything defended while adding pressure. I'd play Be8.

I'd play Nf6.

Nf6 is stronger because it does two useful things at once: it attacks the h5 pawn (which White can’t defend easily) and brings your knight toward the center where it belongs. I'd play Nbd2. You played Bd7 to shuffle, but the real energy in the position comes from fighting for the center; d5 immediately asks White how they’ll handle the tension, and if they take on d5, you recapture with a strong pawn chain that cramps their light-square bishop. It’s a classic advance-to-release idea: push the pawn, and only then decide whether to swap or keep it, based on their reply.

Takeaway: When you see a single advanced enemy pawn, use it as a target to launch your own central break.

I'd play Bf8. You noticed White’s bishop on c5 is lined up with your king—great instinct! But instead of chasing it with the knight (which can get stranded on f5), simply **block the check threat** by tucking your bishop behind the knight on h6; that keeps everything defended and buys time to improve another piece. It’s a calm, safe way to fix your king’s safety without creating new weaknesses.

Takeaway: When an enemy piece eyes your king, start by looking for a *block* or *defender*—not just a chase.

I'd play c5.

You had the right idea with pawn moves, but a5 just sits there while c5 instantly fights for the center and targets the bishop on d4. That bishop is White’s best piece, so chasing it away clarifies Black’s position and paves the way for a smooth ...Rb8–...Bb7–...Qc7 plan to connect the rooks. Since you already considered pushing a pawn, channel that energy into the move that actually threatens something.

Takeaway: In closed positions, look for the pawn break that attacks a defender or opens a file for your pieces.

I'd play Nd8. You tried Nf5 to chase the knight, but the knight just drops back to f3 and White keeps all the play—his queen stays centralized and the bishop on c5 still eyes your weak dark squares. Nd8 reroutes the knight to e6, where it blocks the e-file, defends g7, and threatens to jump to f4 later; meanwhile, it forces White’s queen to decide where she belongs. The idea is to swap plans: instead of pushing pieces away, you’re making yours work together behind your own lines.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s pieces are already active, try repositioning yours to create harmony and long-term threats instead of one-move tricks.

I'd play Nbd7.

You developed a knight, which is good, but look for the *other* knight first. Both knights belong on good squares, and right now the one on b8 can jump to d7 to guard your pawns on c5 and e5 without blocking your queen. That leaves your other knight free to go to c6 or e8 later, so you develop smoothly and keep everything defended.

Takeaway: Always ask “which piece is doing the least?”—develop the one that has the fewest squares first.

I'd play b5. The knight move you chose is fine, but with b5 you grab space on the queenside and challenge White’s pawn on c4—this is how you start a real fight for control. Instead of just developing, you’re saying, “I want to expand where I already have more room,” which is exactly what Black should be doing here. It also keeps your pieces flexible, so you can bring the other knight to d7 or c6 next depending on White’s reply.

Takeaway: When you have more pawns on one side of the board, push to gain space and restrict your opponent before they do the same.

I'd play b5.

You've got a very solid pawn skeleton, but the only real tension is on the queenside. Playing b5 immediately challenges White’s c4 pawn, forcing them to either trade (which gives you a central pawn majority) or move their queen, which disrupts their smooth development. Your move Nc6 was fine, but it didn’t create any lingering pressure—b5 does, and it fits a classic plan at this level: when you have a pawn on c5 and White on c4, pushing b5 is often the most direct way to fight for space and initiative.

Takeaway: When you control the center with pawns, look for the next pawn push to challenge your opponent’s structure and open lines for your pieces.

I'd play **a5**.

You just traded a knight for a knight—fine—but it didn’t really change the position. Notice the pawn on b4 that’s slightly overextended. Pushing a5 challenges it right away, getting White to explain whether b4-b5 is viable. If White just protects with a3, you’ve gained space on the queenside without losing anything. This kind of pawn play is how you make your pieces breathe and keep small advantages alive.

Takeaway: Always ask “what’s the last pawn move my opponent made?”—it often points to the next logical push.

I'd play **Ne5**.

You saw that White’s knight on d5 is strong, but trading knights right away with Nxd5 actually lets White’s bishop on f3 breathe again—it now eyes the weak pawn on g2 and can support a pawn push to e5 later. Instead, **Ne5** kicks the bishop away *now* and keeps your own knight active in the center. If White moves the bishop, you can still consider trading on d5 next turn, but from a stronger position.

Takeaway: When a piece is bothering you, ask: “Can I attack it first and trade later, or trade right away?” — often the first choice keeps more life in your game.

I'd play Bd2.

You played a4, which is a fine move, but it can wait. Right now, Black’s pawn on f4 is sticking out like a sore thumb, and Bd2 does two great things at once: it develops the bishop to a strong diagonal and puts instant pressure on f4. That pawn isn’t going anywhere, so you’re not rushing—you’re just improving your pieces with tempo. After Black castles, you can calmly follow up with moves like a4 or g3, but Bd2 sets the tone: active pieces first, pawn moves second.

Takeaway: When a piece can serve two jobs at once—developing *and* threatening—let it.

I'd play Bd2. The plan is simple: develop a minor piece, aim at that pawn, and tuck your king to safety.

I'd play **Ne5**.

By posting your knight on e5, you attack that bishop while also keeping your own pieces active—your other knight on f6 and your rook on f8 are already well-placed to back it up. The idea is to provoke weaknesses or exchanges that give you more space, like trading queens if White accepts the challenge, or pushing ...f6 next to solidify your center.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong bishop, look for ways to trade it or force it to retreat—especially if you can do so with tempo.

I'd play g3.

Instead, g3 takes direct aim at Black's pawn on f4, which is currently unsupported. This move not only challenges Black's pawn but also prepares to develop your kingside pieces more comfortably, possibly even fianchettoing your bishop later. It’s a simple, principled way to gain space and improve your position without overextending.

Takeaway: When you spot an opponent’s pawn that isn’t well defended, look for ways to challenge it before developing other pieces—it often pays off.

I'd play Bc2. You wanted to shore up b3 and e4, but Rf2 was too passive—the rook just sits there while Black finishes development and starts pushing pawns. Bc2 kicks the annoying knight on b4 *and* connects the rooks behind a flexible pawn chain; if Black recaptures you win two pieces for a rook, and if the knight retreats you’re ready to meet …e5 or …c5 with d5 or f4 at the right moment.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s only active piece is a minor annoyance, drive it away before it becomes a threat.

I'd play Bg5. The move you played, b4, closes the b-file for your rook and lets Black easily shield with a6; Bg5 keeps lines open and keeps Black guessing whether you’ll swing to the kingside next.

Takeaway: When you feel the itch to push a flank pawn, first ask which piece can jump into the action right now.

I'd play Bg5. You played b4, which pushes a pawn but doesn’t actually help your pieces work together. I'd play Bg5.

I'd play **d5**.

I'd play d4.

I'd play Nxd4. You spotted the loose knight on d4, but hiding on h7 ignores the real point: trading it lets you activate your own knight and free your whole position. I'd play Nxd4. You saw the tension on d4 and wanted to safeguard your kingside, but moving the king to h7 just shuffles pieces without a clear plan. I'd play **d4**.

I'd play Ne4. Your queen move to d2 was fine, but it didn’t really challenge Black—it just kept things even. I'd play Nd2. I'd play Nd2.

I'd play Nd2. You already have a good pawn on e5, but the knight on b1 is still just sitting at home—developing it to d2 gets it into the game while keeping everything safe. I'd play Bxb4. You saw a little pawn push (a3), but the board is asking for something bolder: swap off Black’s active bishop before it can cause trouble. I'd play c6. I'd play Rf8. You played d6, which is fine, but it doesn’t really *do* anything—it just reacts to White’s pawn on e5. I'd play **g5**.

I'd play c6.

I'd play O-O. You spotted the pin on the knight, but trading bishop for knight actually helps White: their bishop was awkward while your knight was doing a great job defending the kingside. I'd play O-O.

I'd play g5.

I'd play c3.

I'd play c5. You developed the bishop to d7, which is a fine move—it gets the piece out and keeps things solid—but right now the real battle is in the center. I'd play b5. You played Bxf4, which feels natural—removing a well-placed piece—but it actually gives White free rein in the center. I'd play Rhe1.

I'd play c5.

I'd play Rhe1. You played Nf4, which is fine, but Rhe1 is a more direct way to improve your position. Right now, Black is a little cramped, especially on the kingside, and hasn’t castled yet. I'd play **c5**. You have a strong knight on e4 that needs support, and c5 strikes at White’s d4 pawn while opening the position for your pieces—especially your bishop on c8. I'd play O-O. You spotted a fun check, but your real job right now is to get your king safe behind a wall of pawns—castling does that in one move and brings your rook into the game at the same time. I'd play Ba6+.

I'd play Bd7.

I'd play Ba6+.

I'd play Bxf6. You spotted the hanging pawn on f6 and your instinct to capture it was spot-on. I'd play Bd7. You spotted a sharp check, but Black has a slower, stronger idea: finish development while keeping all the pieces on the board. I'd play Bxf6. You saw the pawn on f6 and correctly grabbed it, but the knight was a blunt tool—after Bxf5 it can’t recapture, so White just picks up our light-squared bishop. I'd play Bxf6. I'd play Rxf2. You spotted the loose knight on f2, but swapping queens let Black recapture with the pawn and keep all their pieces. I'd play Rxf2. I'd play Rxf2. I'd play O-O. Castling first tucks the king to safety, links your rooks, and keeps the tension; after that you can decide whether to take on c5 or use the extra control of d4 to build more pressure. It’s the kind of move that makes every other piece on the board breathe easier.

Takeaway: Always ask “Where is my king safest?” before launching any central skirmish.

I'd play Bxf4.

You spotted the pawn on f4 was loose and took it on instinct—but with the queen, not the bishop. That feels active, yet the queen is worth nine times the pawn, and Black’s knight is ready to jump to e5, kicking her from f4. Instead, drop the “minor” bishop on f4 and suddenly you attack the loose f4 pawn *and* the pawn on d6 behind it, which is anchored only by Black’s own queen. That double-threat keeps the tension in your favor without over-extending your queen.

Takeaway: When a pawn is undefended, reach for a minor piece first to hit it—and look for a second loose target along the same line.

I'd play O-O. You saw the undefended pawn on c5, but grabbing it right away lets White trade knights and tuck the king to safety while your own king stays in the center. Takeaway: When an undefended pawn looks juicy, check first whether your own king is safe—often the right move is castling before snatching the target.

I'd play c4. You noticed that Black’s queen is awkwardly placed on b5, but Rb1 just nudges it to a safer square—after Qa5, it’s still eyeing key squares and we’ve lost time. I'd play c4. I'd play Bxd5. I'd play Qh4+.

I'd play Qh4+.

I'd play Be6.

I'd play Nb4. Black’s bishop on b6 looks scary, but Bb2 says “I’m covering my own back first” while also getting ready to double rooks on the d-file later. It’s simple, safe, and sets up a real plan.

Takeaway: Always ask “can my pieces help each other?” before jumping into the middle.

I'd play Bb2. You played Ne4, which looks active but actually leaves your knight with no good squares—it just gets kicked around or exchanged. Bb2 instead finishes developing your bishop to its strongest diagonal, where it eyes both the weak pawn on d6 and Black’s kingside, while also connecting your rooks. The immediate idea is to follow up with Qc2, lining up a double attack on h7 and keeping the pressure on without overextending.

Takeaway: In calm positions, complete minor-piece development before launching tactical ideas—your pieces work best together.

I'd play Re8.

You noticed White’s pieces are all aiming at your kingside, especially the knight on f5 that’s eyeing h6. Instead of pushing a pawn that doesn’t really change the threat, Re8 steps in front of the queen like a shield and clears a path for your own knight to jump into e5 next move. It’s a calm way to say “I see your plan and I’m ready.”

Takeaway: When the opponent piles up on one side, step a piece in front to block instead of making pawn moves that don’t stop the threat.

I'd play **Re8**. You missed a golden chance to challenge White’s control of the e-file—Re8 immediately contests the open line, supports your queen, and prepares to reroute the knight to e5, where it eyes both the bishop on d3 and the pawn on f3. Instead of pushing a pawn into the dark without clear follow-up, use the rook’s scope to connect your pieces and sharpen the position. The rook move also keeps White’s knight on f5 under indirect fire, tying White down to defense.

Takeaway: When the center is still fluid, activate your rooks along open or half-open files before pushing pawns that can’t be supported.

I'd play **Qd6**.

You chose Rb8, which is fine, but Qd6 is even stronger because it keeps your pieces active and connected. Right now, your queen is a little cut off from the action on d8—moving it to d6 puts her where she can help defend your knight on c6 and your pawn on e5, while also eyeing White’s pawn on h3. It’s like bringing your queen to the main battlefield so she can support your other pieces and maybe even launch an attack later.

Takeaway: Always look for ways to bring your queen into the game without exposing her to danger—placing her where she can help multiple pieces is a great habit!

I'd play Ne5. You saw that d4 creates tension, but pushing too soon just opens lines for White’s pieces—your dark-squared bishop on c5 becomes loose, and White’s own bishop on h4 can swing into action. Instead, Ne5 jumps to a strong outpost, threatens to trade on d3 (which would double White’s pawns and ease your position), and keeps everything defended. It also paves the way for ideas like f6 or a timely ...g6 if White’s knight on f5 starts to loom too large. You’re setting up a plan where your pieces find safe squares while keeping White on the back foot.

Takeaway: Before pushing a pawn, ask if a piece can move to a strong square that improves your position *and* keeps threats alive.

I'd play Qd6. You saw the half-open b-file and wanted to put the rook on it, but right now White has no weaknesses there—marching the a-pawn or getting the queen off the back rank is just better. Qd6 tucks the queen on a safe square where she eyes b4, supports a5 next, and keeps the knight on c6 defended. It also lets you hold the e5 pawn without blocking any pieces.

Takeaway: Before activating a rook, ask if the file is really open enough to matter—often central or pawn moves create better play.

I'd play Be7. You played h5, which doesn’t do much—it doesn’t develop a piece, threaten anything, or really change the position. Be7, by contrast, gives your bishop a good square, connects your rooks so they protect each other, and guards the important pawn on d6. That’s a move that actually improves your position, which is the kind of simple, useful idea beginners should look for first.

Takeaway: Always ask, “Does this move develop a piece or help my pieces work together?”—if not, look for one that does!

I'd play Ba3. You played Ne4, aiming to trade knights and open the f-file, but the knight isn't actually threatening anything right now—it just lands on a square where Black can ignore it or even push ...d5 to kick it back. Ba3, on the other hand, does two powerful things at once: it develops a bishop that’s been sitting idle, and it eyes the weak d6 pawn, which Black has to defend carefully. If Black retreats the bishop with ...Bc7, you can bring your queen to c2 next, keeping an eye on h7 and increasing pressure on d6. This move also keeps the option of pushing e4 open, which could lead to a strong central break later.

Takeaway: When your opponent has weak pawns, try to attack them with pieces before committing to pawn breaks—that way your pieces get active first, and your pawns can follow up even stronger.

I'd play Qd6. You picked Rb8, which isn't bad—it keeps an eye on b2—but the queen belongs on d6 here. It protects your c6 knight and c7 pawn while lifting your rook on a8 out of danger, since White’s queen can’t swing to a4 if your queen’s already on d6. This move also keeps your pieces coordinated and prepares to challenge White’s space advantage by eyeing that juicy e4 pawn indirectly.

Takeaway: At this level, prioritize activating your pieces toward the center with dual-purpose moves—defending key assets while setting up counterplay.

I'd play Be7. You already have a solid pawn structure and developed pieces—now it's time to tuck the bishop somewhere safe where it still guards important squares like d6 and d8. By putting it on e7, you also keep the option to castle later and connect your rooks, which is a big step toward making your position more solid and harder to attack. Your move h5 wasn’t bad, but it doesn’t really improve your position or help coordinate your pieces; Be7 does both.

Takeaway: Focus on developing your pieces to safe squares where they work together, especially before launching pawn moves.

I'd play Bg7. You're in a solid but slightly cramped position, and your dark-squared bishop on f8 is doing very little right now. By developing it to g7, you immediately strengthen your kingside, connect your rooks, and prepare to challenge White's ideas—like the annoying knight jump to d5—while keeping your structure flexible. Your move h5 isn't *wrong*, but it doesn't solve the core issue: your bishop is passive, and passive pieces are a silent killer at your level.

Takeaway: Always ask which of your pieces is doing the least, and improve it before pushing pawns or shuffling.

I'd play c5. You noticed your pieces feel a little cramped, and that’s exactly right—right now White controls more space, especially in the center. Moving the pawn to c5 fights back by kicking that d4 pawn and opening up the game for your pieces, particularly your bishop on c8 and knight on d5. It also gives your queen a new square on c7 where she can support your side of the board more actively, rather than just sitting behind your pawns.

Takeaway: When your position feels tight, look for pawn breaks that challenge your opponent’s center and free your own pieces.

I'd play Bg5. You want to get every piece into the game as soon as you can, and the bishop has been stuck at home since move one. Bg5 puts pressure on the knight that’s defending Black’s king, and it can’t just run away—if Black moves it, you can trade and bring a new pawn closer to promotion. Your king is safe enough for now; developing this bishop helps more than castling right away.

Takeaway: When you’re not sure what to do, look for ways to bring a new piece into the action—especially if it challenges your opponent’s defenders.

I'd play c5. Re8 was harmless, but it doesn’t actually change anything—White can still march the pawn to d5 or e4 whenever they want. Playing c5 strikes right at the center, forcing White to either trade on c5 or push d5, which gives you more space to activate your pieces. It also opens lines for your queen and rooks, making your position more dynamic while shutting down White’s bishop on d3.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s center looks over-extended, challenge it early—don’t wait for them to strengthen it.

I'd play c5. You played Re8, which is a harmless “waiting” rook move, but the board is actually calling for action in the center. By pushing c5, you immediately challenge White’s d4-pawn and create a clear break that can open lines for your pieces. The idea is simple: attack the pawn that holds White’s center together; if White pushes back with d5 you can trade on d4 and activate your knight on d5, or if White takes on c5 you recapture with the b-pawn and keep fighting for the center.

Takeaway: When you’re comfortably developed, look for a central pawn break that fits the pawn structure – it’s often the sharpest way to create real chances.

I'd play Bg5. You wanted to get your king safe with O-O, which is fine, but Black’s knight on f6 is actually blocking their dark-squared bishop—exactly the piece that could give you headaches later. By nudging that knight now, you force Black to react while your center is still solid; after they move the knight you can castle next move, develop the other bishop, and keep the pressure on the b7 pawn without ever giving Black a free tempo.

Takeaway: Look for “free” developing moves that exploit the opponent’s piece placement before you commit to castling.

I'd play **Be3**.

You didn’t do anything wrong on principle—your move just didn’t help your position. The real issue was missing a simple chance to *develop a new piece* while also *defending your d4 pawn* (which Black just attacked with …c5). Be3 does both at once: the bishop steps out, eyes h6, and shields d4 so you can keep building your game. Many beginners stop after developing knights—your bishops are powerful too!

Takeaway: Look for moves that develop a piece *and* defend something important at the same time.

I'd play Qb3. You’re right that kingside castling feels natural, but Black’s pieces are all sitting on dark squares—especially the queen and both bishops. Qb3 instantly eyes the loose b-pawn and keeps the enemy bishop on f8 locked out for one more move, turning Black’s cramped position into a real liability. This move also keeps your own king in the center for now, where it’s safe and even supports a Queeenside expansion like a4 next.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s minor pieces are awkwardly placed, a queen sortie along an open or half-open file often punishes their lack of coordination before they can castle.

I'd play Nf6.

You played queenside castling, which is fine, but the real priority right now is getting your pieces into the game. Nf6 develops your knight toward the center, supports your e4 pawn, and prepares to connect your rooks—something you’ll want if the position opens up later. You missed a chance to activate your forces, and this knight move fixes that while keeping everything safe.

Takeaway: Develop knights toward the center early; they often guard key pawns and set up future plans.

I'd play Ba3. You retreated the bishop to f1, but diagonals are long — putting it on a3 immediately challenges Black’s rook on f8, which is currently doing nothing and has no safe retreat. Instead of hiding, use your pieces to ask questions: the rook will have to move or be traded, giving you open files and lines to keep pressing.

Takeaway: When you have extra space, look for diagonals or files where your opponent’s pieces are awkwardly placed and attack them directly.

I'd play Be3.

You played Bf1, which is fine but a little passive—it doesn’t actually *do* anything. Be3, on the other hand, develops a minor piece, keeps an eye on d4, and sets up a simple plan: get the rooks connected and contest the open e-file. It also prepares to trade bishops if Black plays Bf5, which keeps the position clear and reduces counterplay.

Takeaway: Always ask “What does this move achieve?” before you play it—if the answer is “nothing urgent,” look for something that actively improves your position.

I'd play Be7.

Castling queenside is solid, but it’s a bit slow here—you’re not under direct attack, and White hasn’t committed to pushing b2-b4 yet. By playing Be7, you develop a piece, connect your rooks, and add pressure on that a3 pawn, which is a bit loose. This also keeps the option of kingside castling open for later if the position opens up, giving you flexibility while keeping your position harmonious.

Takeaway: When the position is calm, prioritize improving your pieces and keeping your options open before committing to a single plan.

I'd play Nf6.

You played queenside castling, which is a fine move, but right now your pieces aren't working together. Nf6 is a much better developing move—it brings your knight into the game, supports your center pawn on e4, and makes room for your light-squared bishop to retreat if needed. White’s pawn on a3 isn’t going anywhere, so there’s no rush to attack it. Instead, focus on getting your pieces out and controlling the center.

Takeaway: Always ask yourself, "Which of my pieces is still sitting at home?"—then find the most active square for it.

I'd play Bg4.

You played a4, which nudges the bishop away, but the real opportunity here is to develop your own pieces while keeping an eye on White’s. Bg4 does that perfectly—it brings your bishop out to a strong square and puts a little pressure on White’s knight, which can’t move without losing the pawn on e2. The idea is to keep building your position one piece at a time, not just push pawns without a clear plan. This move also fits neatly into a common beginner theme: trade when you’re ahead in development—here, you might get to swap your bishop for White’s knight, which is usually a good deal for you.

Takeaway: Always look for ways to develop your pieces actively—especially when you can do it while challenging your opponent’s.

I'd play Bg4. You played a4, which tries to kick the bishop away, but White can just retreat to a2 and then you’ve used a tempo on a pawn move instead of developing. Bg4 does two good things at once: it brings your bishop out where it belongs, and it puts pressure on the knight that’s guarding White’s king. If White doesn’t want to trade knights, they’ll have to move the knight again—another tempo for you.

Takeaway: Look for moves that develop a piece while also creating a small threat—don’t settle for just pushing pawns.

I'd play Bg4. You tried *a4* to harass the bishop, but the moment it steps back the pawn on a5 hangs and your a-pawn is stuck backward. *Bg4* does two things at once: it develops your bishop with real bite—pinning the knight to the queen—and it challenges White to spend time saving the pinned piece, letting you follow up with a smooth pawn break like *d4* next move. You’re trading a single move for immediate pressure on the center and a clear plan.

Takeaway: When you can develop with a threat, ask whether the piece you’re attacking can escape without creating a weakness.

I'd play Nf6. You already spotted that developing the knight is the right plan, but Nf6 does it better: it hits the hanging d5-pawn right away while keeping the b6-pawn defended and the kingside flexible. The move also plants the knight on a central square where it can jump to e4 or g4 if White trades bishops on c5, giving you a clear follow-up idea that you can reuse whenever you have a knight on f6.

Takeaway: When you develop a minor piece, aim for the square that tackles the immediate problem (here, the loose pawn) while leaving the most flexible follow-up options.

I'd play Nf6.

Your move Ne7 is fine, but Nf6 is even better because it develops your knight toward the center while also hitting that loose pawn on d5. At your level, always look for ways to activate pieces with a little extra "kick"—here, the knight both gets out and puts pressure on White’s position right away. It also keeps your options open to castle next move or connect your rooks, which is a big goal in these positions.

Takeaway: When developing, ask: “Can this piece also threaten or attack something?” Often the answer is yes, and that makes your move twice as strong.

I'd play h3. You picked Qb3, which is fine, but the real point of the position is to kick that bishop on g4 before it gets too comfortable—Black’s whole bishop pair comes alive if you let it stay. Playing h3 now keeps the initiative light and flexible: the bishop has to move, and once it does you can follow up with Bb3 or castle without worrying about pins or cheap tactics.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a bishop eyeing your knight, ask whether a simple pawn move can challenge it before it causes real trouble.

I'd play Nf6. You already spotted that developing a minor piece is the right idea, but Nf6 does even more: it eyes the d5 pawn (which is currently loose) and gets your knight ready to leap to the central square e4, where it would be very active. The move you chose, Ne7, also develops, but it blocks your dark-squared bishop and doesn’t challenge White’s center as directly—so switching to Nf6 fixes that.

Takeaway: When developing a minor piece, place it where it attacks key central squares or loose pawns while keeping your other pieces free.

I'd play h3.

You played Qb3, which is a fine move, but it doesn’t *do* much right now—Black’s bishop on g4 isn’t going anywhere yet, and the queen can always go there later. Here, h3 is a simple, strong move that asks Black a clear question: *"What are you doing with that bishop?"* It’s a small pawn move that starts a concrete plan—kick the bishop, then finish developing with moves like Bb3 or O-O. At your level, forcing your opponent to make decisions (like whether to retreat or trade) is a great way to build pressure without overcomplicating things.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s piece is loose or eyeing your position, ask yourself: *Can I challenge it with a pawn first?*

I'd play O-O. You spotted that your knight on d4 is under attack, but instead of retreating it, you can use this moment to finally get your king to safety—castling is one of the first things you learn to do early, and here it’s still the best move. After you castle, you’ll connect your rooks and your king is tucked away, so you can start thinking about pushing your pawns or moving other pieces forward safely.

Takeaway: When a piece is threatened, first check if castling or developing another piece can solve the problem without retreating.

I'd play f4.

You didn’t blunder anything with Be3, but it misses a golden opportunity to strike while Black’s king is still in the center. By pushing f4, you’re saying, “I want to open the f-file and challenge the e5 pawn—if Black takes, my queen swings to f3 with real pressure on f7, and my rooks suddenly see daylight.” It’s a classic advanced idea: when the opponent hasn’t castled, pawn breaks in the center or kingside are often right, even if they feel a bit loosening. You’d be trading activity for structure, and at your level, that’s almost always the correct trade.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s king is stuck in the center, opening lines with pawn breaks beats waiting for perfect harmony.

I'd play O-O. You spotted how the knight on d4 eyes f3 and ties White’s bishop to the defence of e4, but parking it on c6 just drifts without purpose. Castling immediately tucks the king, activates the rook, and keeps the central tension; if White trades on d4 your e-pawn recaptures and your e7-bishop breathes.

Takeaway: When you have a harmonious developing move that improves king safety and activates a rook, do it before side-stepping a central piece.

I'd play **c6**. You played Nc6, which isn’t bad, but c6 does something more concrete: it kicks the bishop on c4 while keeping your knight on d4 active and your structure flexible. After c6, White will likely trade bishops on d4, leaving you with a clean, central pawn duo on d4 and e5 — perfect for controlling the center and threatening to push forward. This move also keeps your pieces connected and your king safe, so you can castle later without worries.

Takeaway: When a bishop eyes your weak pawns, look for a pawn move that challenges it while keeping your position flexible and your pieces defended.

I'd play Qg6. You saw there was some pressure, but Qe6 just walked into a pin—your queen was staring down White’s rook, and that’s an easy trap to miss when you’re not checking which enemy pieces are lined up. Qg6 steps off the board’s danger lines while hitting the weak pawn on g2 that White can’t really defend without awkward moves, and it keeps your own pawn on e4 safe too. The plan is simple: put your queen where she’s both active and safe, and keep an eye out for any squares where your pieces might be lining up for enemy rooks or bishops.

Takeaway: When your queen is exposed, move her to a safe square that still helps your position—attack something or defend a weak spot, don’t just retreat.

I'd play Ba2. You saw the bishop on c4 was staring at the weak f7 pawn, but moving it to e3 just blocked the queen’s view and didn’t really threaten anything. Ba2 keeps the eye on f7 while opening the diagonal for your queen, so if Black tries to defend with …Be6 you can simply take on e6 and get a solid pawn-up position. It’s a quiet move that actually ramps up the pressure—something beginners often overlook when they’re hunting for flashy checks or captures.

Takeaway: When your pieces share the same target, pull one back to let the other join the attack.

I'd play **Qg6**.

You were right to think about bringing the queen back to help defend, but Qe6 just blocks your own rook and doesn’t actually guard anything useful. Qg6, on the other hand, does two simple things: it attacks White’s pawn on g2—tying down their pieces to defend it—and keeps your pawn on e4 safe at the same time. That gives you time to finish developing your light-squared bishop or move your rooks to the center without worrying about immediate tricks.

Takeaway: When your queen is attacked, look for squares where she both attacks and defends something you already have.

I'd play Qf5. You were right to look at the knights, but Qe6 walked into a pin and dropped a pawn. Qf5 hits both knights at once, and if White grabs the e4 pawn you simply take the d5 knight back with the c6 knight—no hang, no pin. It also keeps the queen active where most of the pieces are.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s minor pieces are floating, train your queen on both of them instead of just defending.

I'd play f4.

You wanted to develop your bishop, but Be3 doesn’t really challenge Black—your light-squared bishop is already doing a good job pressuring f7, and f4 fights straight for the center while opening the f-file for your rook. After f4 exf4, your queen can jump to f3, eyeing both the weak f7 pawn and the undefended bishop on d4, which suddenly feels awkward. This move turns a quiet-looking position into an open fight where you dictate the terms.

Takeaway: When you see a chance to pry open the center, go for it—especially if your pieces are ready to pounce right after.

I'd play d4.

You played c3, which does threaten the knight, but it lets White trade on c4 next move and reach a pretty balanced position. Instead, d4 immediately challenges the bishop on e3—White has to decide whether to retreat or exchange, and either choice gives you a clear follow-up. If they retreat to f4, you can reply with c3, hitting that same knight while supporting your center. The idea is to keep the tension and make White react to your strong pawn moves, rather than letting them simplify.

Takeaway: When you have active pawns in the center, use them to challenge your opponent’s pieces and force decisions.

I'd play d4.

Your move c3 isn’t *wrong*, but it’s passive—you pushed a pawn without a clear follow-up. d4 strikes right at the heart of White’s position: it chases the bishop that’s eyeing your kingside and suddenly your center looks much healthier. After d4, you’ll feel the board opening up for your pieces, and you’ll have real targets instead of just making a move to “do something.” Plus, you’re hitting back where White is a bit overextended, which is always satisfying.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a piece sticking out too far, look for a pawn break that kicks it away and gives your own pieces more room.

I'd play Bxa3. You saw the chance to push c3, which is good—it’s active and puts pressure on White’s position. But Bxa3 does two even bigger things at once: it grabs a weak pawn on the queenside (the a3 pawn is undefended and far from White’s king) and sets up a discovered attack on the rook on f8 once the bishop moves. This keeps the initiative while also improving your own position by trading a passive bishop for a vital pawn. The pawn on a3 is a real target here:White’s queenside is a bit loose, and grabbing it right away punishes that.

Takeaway: When you spot a pawn that’s weak and undefended on the flank, look for ways to grab it before your opponent can defend it—often, that pawn is worth more than just its face value.

I'd play Qc7. You played g6, which weakens the dark squares around your king—a risky move when White still has both bishops and a knight that can target those squares. Qc7 does two useful things at once: it guards your rook on b8 (which was unprotected) and puts pressure on White’s pawn on e5, asking White to defend it while keeping your pieces coordinated. The move also keeps your queen active and connected to your king, giving you a flexible setup for castling next move.

Takeaway: Always check for moves that defend weak points and create a threat at the same time—it’s the easiest way to keep your game balanced.

I'd play h3.

You spotted the undefended bishop on g4, but instead of snatching a pawn you could have chased the piece first. h3 asks the bishop, “Are you serious?” If it retreats to h5 or f5, we can follow up with g4 and gain space. Even if Black swaps on e2, trading his bishop for our knight, we recapture and stay solid while clarifying the kingside. This kind of move keeps the tension without giving Black loose targets.

Takeaway: When you see an enemy piece that looks loose, ask yourself, “Can I attack it cheaply before committing to a capture?”

I'd play Qc7. You missed a quick chance to challenge White’s space advantage: Qc7 immediately hits the pawn on e5, which is only defended by the bishop on d2. That forces White to either trade on d7 (which activates your knight) or move the bishop and give up the pawn. At the advanced level, spotting these “free rolls” is what separates solid players from those who drift into passive positions.

Takeaway: When a central pawn has only one defender, look for ways to attack it with your own pieces before committing to slow pawn moves.

I'd play Qc7. You spotted that White’s pawn on **c5** is loose and is only defended by the queen — great tactical eye! But instead of trying to snatch it right away with g6 (which leaves your own king stuck in the center and walks into a nasty pin), Qc7 keeps the pressure on while also buying you time to castle. The idea is simple: attack the **e5** pawn next, which may force White to defend it with f4 and create new targets, or even let you grab the c5 pawn safely later.

Takeaway: Always ask, “What’s the simplest threat I can add while I’m also defending?” — it often gives you a free move.

I'd play h3. The bishop on g4 is eyeing your kingside, and while right now it's not doing much, it could become a headache if you castle next move—Black could trade it for your knight on f3, leaving your pawn structure weak. Driving it back with h3 keeps your pieces flexible and prepares a safe kingside castle. You noticed the idea of defending with Qb3, but that moves your queen before developing, which can give Black free tempo moves later.

Takeaway: Always ask "What's my opponent's most annoying piece?"—then kick it before it kicks you.

I'd play h3.

You just grabbed the pawn on e4, but Black’s knight was undefended for a good reason: it was pinned to their queen. After you took it, Black recaptured with the bishop and won your pawn back, leaving you with nothing. Instead of snatching material, h3 gives the bishop on g4 a gentle nudge so you can get your pawn back next move and keep everything safe. It’s a simple way to remind yourself that pieces that look loose may still be protected—just not always in the way you first see.

Takeaway: Always double-check why a piece is undefended before you take it.

I'd play Bg4+.

You spotted a loose piece (the pawn on f2), but going for material right away with Nxb2 lets White wriggle free. Instead, start with check—Bg4+—which forces White to block with f3, giving you time to win the b2 pawn next move with tempo. This teaches you the power of using threats to set up your own plans rather than grabbing the first pawn you see. The bishop also helps defend your knight on d3, keeping everything safe.

Takeaway: Always ask “What does my opponent have to do right now?” before grabbing material.

I'd play Bg4+.

You spotted the loose knight on d3, but grabbing a pawn with Nxb2 misses a golden chance to stir up real trouble. Bg4+ forces White’s king to move—f3 is pretty much forced—and suddenly the f2-pawn is hanging, the queen is staring at your knight, and your whole position feels alive again. That check also buys you time to grab the b2-pawn next move with real venom, since White is too busy dealing with the king.

Takeaway: When you see a loose piece, ask “Can I hit something bigger first?” before pocketing the free pawn.

I'd play Nxf2.

You already spotted the tempting fork on b2, but the real sting is on f2—the knight lands and immediately threatens the queen *and* the rook on h1. After White moves the queen, your other bishop can slide to g4 with tempo, forcing White to weaken their kingside pawns. Capturing on f2 transforms your knight from a passive piece into a real aggressor, and the follow-up check on g4 teaches you to always look for a second punch.

Takeaway: When a fork hits two major pieces, ask which version lets you keep pressing the attack afterwards.

I'd play Ng4. You saw the undefended rook on a1 and grabbed it, which is a natural impulse—but right now, your knight is the only piece attacking the White king’s safety. Moving it to g4 piles on pressure where it matters most: it eyes the weak pawns in front of White’s king, forces White to scramble, and still lets you take the rook on a1 next move if nothing better appears.

Takeaway: Always ask, “Can I check, threaten, or defend near the enemy king?” before capturing a stray piece.

I'd play **fxg5**.

You spotted that the knight on g5 is hanging—great eye! By taking it with the pawn, you win a piece right away, and White’s queen has to come out to recapture, giving you time to lift your rook to g8 to guard your own knight. This fixes your mistake of playing h5, which just pushes a pawn and leaves the knight still loose; instead, just take what your opponent didn’t protect.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended piece, ask “Can I take it safely?”—if the answer is yes, do it and then look for your next active move.

I'd play fxg5. You saw the knight on g5 hanging, but instead of kicking it with h5—a move that just lets White keep attacking your knight—take it right now! Every piece traded brings you closer to the endgame, where your pawn structure and active rooks will matter more than White’s loose pieces. After fxg5, you threaten to take back with the queen and snap up the e4 pawn, so White has to scramble just to keep material equal.

Takeaway: When you see an unprotected piece, ask yourself: can I win it without losing mine, and does trading help my position?

I'd play **Qd7**. I'd play Rd5. You wanted to challenge the d4 pawn, but your move c5 actually puts another pawn *in front* of it—almost like volunteering to block your own pieces! The rook on d5 sits right on that open file, threatening to take the d4 pawn and also keeping your f5 pawn safe; simple, active, and easy to remember. Just imagine sliding your rook forward like a bulldozer: it clears the way and lets your other pieces breathe.

Takeaway: Always put a piece on a square where it attacks something *and* defends one of your own guys.

I'd play **Qc7**.

You played c5, which weakens your pawn structure and gives up control of d5—a key central square. Instead, **Qc7** keeps the tension while eyeing the weak h2 pawn and preparing to reroute your knight (say, Na6 or Nd7) to a better square. The queen is safe on c7, and the move subtly improves your coordination without committing to anything rash. You’re still solid, and White has no immediate breakthroughs—just the kind of patient, high-level play that wins games at your level.

Takeaway: When the center is tense, ask what your pieces *want* to do—not what pawns *can* do.

I'd play Nd4. You grabbed the pawn on c4, but this just lets White castle and grab the initiative—at your level, you want to keep the tension and punish little positional concessions. Nd4 pressures the knight on e2 and threatens to swap off White’s good bishop by sliding your knight to f3; if they don’t react precisely, you can even pick up the h3 pawn next move. Instead of immediately cashing in, you’re asking White awkward questions and building a reusable knight-outpost habit.

Takeaway: When you see a pawn that looks free, first check if leaping a piece to a strong square tangles the opponent’s minor pieces.

I'd play h3.

You played Bd2, which is fine, but it doesn’t really *do* much—it just moves the bishop without a clear follow-up. h3, on the other hand, is a simple, direct move with a clear purpose: it chases the bishop on g4, which is pinning your knight. If Black trades bishops, you recapture with your queen, which activates her and keeps your knight safe. This also helps you avoid little tricks Black might try with the pinned knight.

Takeaway: When your opponent pins a piece, look for ways to break the pin before worrying about other plans.

I'd play Qb6.

You grabbed the pawn right away, but White can safely castle and the bishop on c1 suddenly eyes your king from a distance. Instead, Qb6 eyeballs that b2-pawn—it’s only guarded by the queen—and also keeps an eye on your own pawn on a7. If White takes on d5, your bishop recaptures and still nags that b2-pawn, often forcing White to retreat rather than castle into instant trouble. The pattern of using the queen to double-attack undefended or loosely guarded points is one you’ll see again and again.

Takeaway: Before you take a pawn, ask which of your pieces can also create a new threat while keeping your position solid.

I'd play h3. You developed most of your pieces already — now it's time to kick that annoying bishop on g4 away before it lands a pin on your knight. Driving the bishop to h5 or forcing it to trade on f3 keeps the initiative, and trading there actually helps your pawn structure because doubled pawns are only a weakness if the enemy can target them. The move you played, Bd2, is fine, but it doesn’t challenge anything in Black’s camp; this is a moment to ask the question, “Can I annoy my opponent a little?”

Takeaway: When your opponent plants a piece in your half of the board, consider pushing a pawn to chase it — it’s often simpler than developing another piece and keeps them on the defensive.

I'd play Be3. You went with Bd2, which is fine, but Be3 does more: it eyes the weak pawn on h6 while also preparing to swing the rook to the semi-open c-file. That simple piece shuffle (Bishop first, then rook) creates real pressure on the queenside without giving Black any loose targets to hit back at. You already spotted the idea of challenging h6 with your bishop; Be3 just does it while keeping the a2 knight defended and the f2 pawn safe too.

I'd play **Rh2**.

Instead, **Rh2** is simple and safe: it tucks the rook behind your pawn wall, guards the knight on f2, and keeps everything defended. You’re not giving Black any easy targets, and the rook is ready to switch to the open h-file if they push h4 later.

Takeaway: When nothing is under attack, first ask, “Which move keeps my pieces safest?” and look for quiet ways to guard weak spots.

I'd play Rh2. Takeaway: When your opponent is eyeing a pawn break, ask which of your pieces can shuffle over to stop it without creating new weaknesses.

I'd play **b5**.

Instead of moving the queen out of the way, kicking the bishop with **b5** is much stronger: it forces White to retreat and gives you space to develop your pieces comfortably. You also keep the tension in the center, which puts pressure on White to make careful moves.

I'd play **Rh2**.

The rook also frees f2 for your knight, giving you more flexibility to reroute pieces if Black tries something tricky on the queenside.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a pawn storm coming, first ask “What can I defend *without* opening lines?” — often the calmest move is the strongest.

I'd play **d5**.

I'd play Qf5. I'd play d5.

I'd play Qf5.

I'd play Re1. I'd play Qf5. The idea is simple but powerful: your queen steps into a great square where she both threatens Black’s f7 pawn and eyes the knight on d7, tying down Black’s pieces. I'd play Re1. Instead of launching an early check that gives Black an easy developing move (Nfd7), swing the rook to the open e-file to pressure Black’s knight on e5. I'd play Re1. I'd play d5.

I'd play Bb3. You're already doing a great job developing pieces and controlling the center, but Bb3 focuses on a classic target—the f7 pawn. I'd play Bd5.

I'd play Bd5. I'd play d6. You played h6 to chase the bishop, but at this level you should ask: *what is White’s real threat*? I'd play c4. I'd play Bxc4.

I'd play Qe6. Your move Rad8 didn’t help your position at all—it just shuffled a piece without any clear plan. I'd play Nf3. You chose c3, which is a little too eager to strike at d4—it actually hands Black a tempo after dxc3, letting them open the center when their pieces are already well posted. I'd play Qh4. I'd play Nd4. You just dropped a full rook by playing Rad8, which left the knight on c6 hanging—always double-check that your non-pawn moves defend every piece you already have on the board. I'd play Nd4. You saw that White’s queen is on c4, eyeing weaknesses on the kingside, but you put a rook on d8 where it doesn’t change anything—here, the knight belongs on d4. I'd play axb5. You played a5, which just hangs the pawn without any real reason—it doesn’t help your pieces or stop Black’s plans. I'd play **axb5**. You played a5, which gives Black a free tempo to grab space with ...b4 or strike back in the center — that’s like handing them the initiative. I'd play Nf3. You played c3, which just pushes the d4-pawn without really improving your position—but Nf3 actually puts pressure on that pawn while also eyeing e5, a key central square. That knight also protects your kingside and helps you castle next move, which you’ll want to do soon to keep your king safe. I'd play a5.

I'd play Nc6.

I'd play d4.

I'd play Qg4. Your move Qf2 was a safe-looking retreat, but it just stayed put without threatening anything. I'd play Qg4.

I'd play Rc5. You noticed your rook was under attack and wanted to save it—great instinct! I'd play **Bd5**.

I'd play Bxe6.

I'd play Qg4. You spotted that your queen is attacked, but defending it with Qf2 gave Black’s bishop on e6 a free tempo to jump into h4 later, costing you momentum. I'd play Rc5. You spotted the pressure on the c4 pawn, but instead of just nudging the rook to the side (Rc7), you can do something much sharper: trade rooks on d5 and then swing your queen to a7. I'd play Re8+.

I'd play Rc5.

I'd play Ne4. I'd play Ne4. You played Qe2, which controls the diagonal but doesn’t create any immediate pressure—it lets Black stay comfortable. I'd play Re8+.

I'd play Nd4.

I'd play Rb8.

If White brings the queen to b4, you’re ready with b5 next move, and now the queen can’t easily retreat because the rook is already on b8.

I'd play Bb5. You played d6, which does nothing—it just nudges the bishop away without improving your position. This kind of pin against a major piece is a classic way to build pressure without over-extending, and it’s exactly the kind of move that wins at your level when opponents misplay the coordination of their pieces.

Takeaway: When you spot a pin against a major piece (rook or queen), it’s often worth playing—it ties their pieces down and can force concessions.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s queen eyes loose pawns, look first for ways to *improve* a piece, not just push pawns—knights love squares where they cover both center and weaknesses.
